**Shanghai**[[a]](#cite_note-13) is a [direct-administered municipality](/wiki/Direct-administered_municipality "Direct-administered municipality") and the [most populous urban area](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_China_by_population "List of cities in China by population") in [China](/wiki/China "China"). It has a population of 29,558,908 in the urban area as of 2025. The city is located on the [Chinese](/wiki/East_China "East China") shoreline on the southern [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") of the [Yangtze River](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River"), with the [Huangpu River](/wiki/Huangpu_River "Huangpu River") bisecting the city. Shanghai is a global center for finance, business and economics, research, science and technology, manufacturing, transportation, [tourism](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_Shanghai "List of tourist attractions in Shanghai"), and [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Shanghai "Culture of Shanghai"). The [Port of Shanghai](/wiki/Port_of_Shanghai "Port of Shanghai") is the [world's busiest container port](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_container_port "World's busiest container port").[[13]](#cite_note-14) As of 2022, the Greater Shanghai metropolitan area was estimated to produce a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") ([nominal](/wiki/GDP_(nominal) "GDP (nominal)")) of nearly 13 trillion [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") ($1.9 trillion).[[14]](#cite_note-GDP2022-15)

Originally a [fishing village](/wiki/Fishing_village "Fishing village") and market town, Shanghai grew to global prominence in the 19th century due to domestic and foreign trade and its favorable port location. The city was one of five [treaty ports](/wiki/Treaty_ports "Treaty ports") forced to open to trade with the [Europeans](/wiki/Europeans "Europeans") after the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"), with the [Shanghai International Settlement](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Settlement "Shanghai International Settlement") and [French Concession](/wiki/Shanghai_French_Concession "Shanghai French Concession") subsequently established. The city became a primary commercial and financial hub of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") in the 1930s. During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), it was the site of the [Battle of Shanghai](/wiki/Battle_of_Shanghai "Battle of Shanghai"). This was followed by the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") with the [Communists taking over the city](/wiki/Shanghai_Campaign "Shanghai Campaign") and most of the mainland. During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), trade was mostly limited to other socialist countries in the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc"), causing the city's global influence to decline.

The [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up") supported by [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") led to extensive redevelopment by the 1990s, particularly in the [Pudong New Area](/wiki/Pudong_New_Area "Pudong New Area"), spurring the return of finance and foreign investment. The city has re-emerged as a hub for international trade and finance. It is the home of the [Shanghai Stock Exchange](/wiki/Shanghai_Stock_Exchange "Shanghai Stock Exchange"), [the largest stock exchange in the Asia-Pacific](/wiki/List_of_major_stock_exchanges "List of major stock exchanges") by [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") and the [Shanghai Free-Trade Zone](/wiki/Shanghai_Free-Trade_Zone "Shanghai Free-Trade Zone"), the first [free-trade zone](/wiki/Free-trade_zone "Free-trade zone") in mainland China. It is ranked eighth globally on the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index"). Shanghai has been classified as an Alpha+ ([global first-tier](/wiki/Global_city "Global city")) city by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network"). As of 2024, it is home to 13 companies of the [*Fortune* Global 500](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500#Fortune_Global_500_of_2021 "Fortune Global 500")—the fourth-highest number of any city.[[15]](#cite_note-FG-16) Shanghai is the world's second [largest city by scientific outputs](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_scientific_output "List of cities by scientific output") and home to several [highly ranked universities](/wiki/Double_First-Class_Construction "Double First-Class Construction"), including [Fudan](/wiki/Fudan_University "Fudan University"), [Shanghai Jiao Tong](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiao_Tong_University "Shanghai Jiao Tong University") and [Tongji](/wiki/Tongji_University "Tongji University").[[16]](#cite_note-17) The [Shanghai Metro](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro "Shanghai Metro"), first opened in 1993, is the [largest metro network](/wiki/List_of_metro_systems "List of metro systems") in the world by route length.

Shanghai has been described as a global finance and innovation hub, and it is one of the ten biggest economic hubs in the world. Featuring several architectural styles such as [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") and [shikumen](/wiki/Shikumen "Shikumen"), the city contains the [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui "Lujiazui") skyline, and museums and historic buildings such as the [City God Temple](/wiki/City_God_Temple_of_Shanghai "City God Temple of Shanghai"), [Yu Garden](/wiki/Yu_Garden "Yu Garden"), the [China Pavilion](/wiki/China_Pavilion "China Pavilion") and buildings along [the Bund](/wiki/The_Bund "The Bund"). Shanghai is known for its [cuisine](/wiki/Shanghai_cuisine "Shanghai cuisine"), [local language](/wiki/Shanghainese "Shanghainese"), and cosmopolitan culture. It ranks sixth in the [list of cities with the most skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_cities_with_the_most_skyscrapers "List of cities with the most skyscrapers").

The two [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character") in the city's name are [上](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A "wikt:上") (*shàng*/*zaon*, "upon") and [海](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B5%B7 "wikt:海") (*hǎi*/*hé*, "sea"), together meaning "On the Sea". The earliest occurrence of this name is the 11th-century [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), when there was a river confluence and a town with this name in the area. Others contend that the city is referenced in historical records dating back 2150 years, and that its ancient name, "Hu", suggests it was a fishing village. In 1280 it was renamed "Shanghai", which translates to "Above the Sea".[[17]](#cite_note-18) The name's interpretation was disputed, but Chinese historians concluded that during the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), the area of modern-day Shanghai was under sea level, so the land appeared to be "on the sea".[[18]](#cite_note-Danielson,_Eric_N._2004,_pp.8-9-19)

Shanghai is officially abbreviated [沪](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B2%AA "wikt:沪")[[b]](#cite_note-21) (*Hù*/*wu*) in [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), a contraction of [沪渎](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B2%AA%E6%B8%8E "wikt:沪渎")[[c]](#cite_note-23) (*Hù Dú*/*wu-doq*, "[Harpoon](/wiki/Harpoon "Harpoon") Ditch"), a 4th- or 5th-century [Jin](/wiki/Jin_Dynasty_(265%E2%80%93420) "Jin Dynasty (265–420)") name for the mouth of [Suzhou Creek](/wiki/Suzhou_Creek "Suzhou Creek") when it was the main conduit into the ocean.[[21]](#cite_note-Shenhu_Origin-24) This character appears on motor vehicle license plates issued in the municipality.[[22]](#cite_note-25)

### Alternative names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Alternative names")]

[申](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%94%B3 "wikt:申") (*Shēn*/*sén*) or [申](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%94%B3 "wikt:申")[城](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%9F%8E "wikt:城") (*Shēnchéng*/*sén-zen*, "Shen City") was an early name originating from [Lord Chunshen](/wiki/Lord_Chunshen "Lord Chunshen"), a 3rd-century BC nobleman and prime minister of the [state of Chu](/wiki/Chu_(state) "Chu (state)"), whose [fief](/wiki/Fief "Fief") included modern Shanghai.[[21]](#cite_note-Shenhu_Origin-24) [华](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8D%8E "wikt:华")[亭](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%AD "wikt:亭")[[d]](#cite_note-26) (*Huátíng*/*gho-din*) was another early name for Shanghai. In AD 751, [Huating County](/wiki/Songjiang,_Shanghai "Songjiang, Shanghai") was established as the first [county-level administration](/wiki/County_(China) "County (China)") within modern-day Shanghai by [Zhao Juzhen](/wiki/Zhao_Juzhen "Zhao Juzhen"), the governor of [Wu Commandery](/wiki/Wu_Commandery "Wu Commandery").[[23]](#cite_note-27)

[魔](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%AD%94 "wikt:魔")[都](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%83%BD "wikt:都") (*Módū*/*mó-tu*, "monster/fiend/magical city"),[[e]](#cite_note-28) is a contemporary nickname for Shanghai.[[24]](#cite_note-29) The name was first mentioned in *Mato* (1924) by Japanese novelist [Shōfu Muramatsu](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dfu_Muramatsu "Shōfu Muramatsu").[[25]](#cite_note-30) The city has various English nicknames including the "[New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") of China", in reference to its status as a cosmopolitan [megalopolis](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") and [financial hub](/wiki/Financial_hub "Financial hub"),[[26]](#cite_note-31) the "Pearl of the Orient", and the "Paris of the East".[[27]](#cite_note-paris_of_the_east-32)[[28]](#cite_note-33)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Shanghai](/wiki/History_of_Shanghai "History of Shanghai")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Shanghai](/wiki/Timeline_of_Shanghai "Timeline of Shanghai").

### Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Antiquity")]

The western part of modern-day Shanghai was inhabited 6,000 years ago.[[29]](#cite_note-34) During the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period") (approximately 771 to 476 BC), it belonged to the [Kingdom of Wu](/wiki/Wu_(state) "Wu (state)"), which was conquered by the [Kingdom of Yue](/wiki/Yue_(state) "Yue (state)"), which in turn was conquered by the Kingdom of Chu.[[30]](#cite_note-ancient-35) During the [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") (475 BC), Shanghai was part of the fief of [Lord Chunshen](/wiki/Lord_Chunshen "Lord Chunshen") of Chu, one of the [Four Lords of the Warring States](/wiki/Four_Lords_of_the_Warring_States "Four Lords of the Warring States"). Local legends claim he ordered the excavation of the [Huangpu River](/wiki/Huangpu_River "Huangpu River"), an important river in the area. Its former or poetic name, the Chunshen River, gave Shanghai its nickname of "Shēn".[[30]](#cite_note-ancient-35) Fishermen living in the Shanghai area then created a fish tool called the *hù*, which lent its name to the outlet of Suzhou Creek north of the [Old City](/wiki/Old_City_of_Shanghai "Old City of Shanghai") and became a common nickname and [abbreviation](/wiki/Chinese_abbreviations "Chinese abbreviations") for the city.[[31]](#cite_note-36)

### Imperial era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Imperial era")]

During the Tang and Song dynasties, Qinglong Town (青龙镇[[f]](#cite_note-37)) in modern [Qingpu District](/wiki/Qingpu_District "Qingpu District") was a major trading port. Established in 746, it developed into what was historically called a "giant town of the Southeast". The port experienced thriving trade with provinces along the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") and the Chinese coast, as well as foreign countries such as Japan and [Silla](/wiki/Silla "Silla").[[1]](#cite_note-kaogu-1) By the end of the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), the center of trading had moved downstream of the [Wusong River](/wiki/Wusong_River "Wusong River") to Shanghai.[[32]](#cite_note-38) Its status was upgraded from a village to a market town in 1074; in 1172, a second sea wall was built to stabilize the ocean coastline, supplementing an earlier dike.[[33]](#cite_note-39) From the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") in 1292 until Shanghai officially became a [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") in 1927, central Shanghai was administered as a county under [Songjiang Prefecture](/wiki/Songjiang,_Shanghai "Songjiang, Shanghai"), which had its seat in the present-day [Songjiang District](/wiki/Songjiang_District "Songjiang District").[[34]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Old_City_of_Shanghai_will_walls_and_seafront.jpg/250px-Old_City_of_Shanghai_will_walls_and_seafront.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_City_of_Shanghai_will_walls_and_seafront.jpg)

The [Old City of Shanghai](/wiki/Old_City_of_Shanghai "Old City of Shanghai") in the 17th century

Shanghai's first [city wall](/wiki/City_wall "City wall") was built in 1554 to protect the town from raids by [Japanese pirates](/wiki/Wokou "Wokou"). It was 10 m (33 ft) high and 5 km (3 mi) in circumference. A [City God Temple](/wiki/City_God_Temple_of_Shanghai "City God Temple of Shanghai") was built in 1602 during the [Wanli](/wiki/Wanli_Emperor "Wanli Emperor") reign. This honor was usually reserved for prefectural capitals and not normally given to a county seat like Shanghai. Scholars theorized that this reflected the town's economic importance.[[35]](#cite_note-Danielson,_Eric_N._2004,_p.10-41)

During the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), two central government policy changes caused Shanghai to become one of the most important seaports in the [Yangtze Delta](/wiki/Yangtze_Delta "Yangtze Delta") region. The first was in 1684, when the [Kangxi Emperor](/wiki/Kangxi_Emperor "Kangxi Emperor") reversed the 1525 prohibition on oceangoing vessels. In 1732, the [Qianlong Emperor](/wiki/Qianlong_Emperor "Qianlong Emperor") moved the customs office for [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") province ([江](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B1%9F#Simplified_Chinese "wikt:江")[海](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B5%B7#Simplified_Chinese "wikt:海")[关](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%B3#Simplified_Chinese "wikt:关");[[g]](#cite_note-42) see [Customs House, Shanghai](/wiki/Customs_House,_Shanghai "Customs House, Shanghai")) from [Songjiang](/wiki/Songjiang,_Shanghai "Songjiang, Shanghai") to Shanghai, and gave Shanghai exclusive control over customs collections for Jiangsu's foreign trade. Shanghai became the major trade port for the lower Yangtze region by 1735, despite being at the lowest administrative level in the political hierarchy.[[36]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Shanghai_1884.jpg/250px-Shanghai_1884.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_1884.jpg)

A map of Shanghai in 1884; Chinese area are in yellow, French in red/pink, British in blue, American in orange.

In the 19th century, international attention and recognition of its economic and trade potential at the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") grew.[[37]](#cite_note-44) British forces occupied the city during the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War").[[38]](#cite_note-45) The war ended in 1842 with the [Treaty of Nanking](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking"), which opened Shanghai as one of the five [treaty ports](/wiki/Treaty_ports "Treaty ports") for international trade.[[39]](#cite_note-46) The [Treaty of the Bogue](/wiki/Treaty_of_the_Bogue "Treaty of the Bogue"), the [Treaty of Wanghia](/wiki/Treaty_of_Wanghia "Treaty of Wanghia"), and the [Treaty of Whampoa](/wiki/Treaty_of_Whampoa "Treaty of Whampoa"), signed between 1843 and 1844, forced Chinese concession to European and American desires for visitation and trade in China. Britain, France, and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") established a presence outside the walled city of Shanghai, which remained under the direct administration of the Chinese.[[40]](#cite_note-SHChronicles-47)

The Chinese-held Old City of Shanghai fell to rebels from the [Small Swords Society](/wiki/Small_Swords_Society "Small Swords Society") in 1853, but was regained by the Qing government in February 1855.[[41]](#cite_note-48) In 1854, the [Shanghai Municipal Council](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_Council "Shanghai Municipal Council") was created to manage the foreign settlements. Between 1860 and 1862, the [Taiping rebels](/wiki/Taiping_Rebellion "Taiping Rebellion") [twice attacked](/wiki/Battle_of_Shanghai_(1861) "Battle of Shanghai (1861)") Shanghai and destroyed the city's eastern and southern suburbs, but failed to take the city.[[42]](#cite_note-WellWilli-49) In 1863, the British settlement south of [Suzhou Creek](/wiki/Suzhou_Creek "Suzhou Creek") (northern [Huangpu District](/wiki/Huangpu_District,_Shanghai "Huangpu District, Shanghai")) and the American settlement to the north (southern [Hongkou District](/wiki/Hongkou_District "Hongkou District")) joined to form the [Shanghai International Settlement](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Settlement "Shanghai International Settlement"). The French opted out of the [Shanghai Municipal Council](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Settlement "Shanghai International Settlement") and maintained [its own concession](/wiki/Shanghai_French_Concession "Shanghai French Concession") at the city's south and southwest.[[43]](#cite_note-50) The [First Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/First_Sino-Japanese_War "First Sino-Japanese War") concluded with the 1895 [Treaty of Shimonoseki](/wiki/Treaty_of_Shimonoseki "Treaty of Shimonoseki"), which elevated Japan as another foreign power in Shanghai. Japan built the first factories in Shanghai, which were copied by other foreign powers. This international activity gave Shanghai the nickname "the Great [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") of China".[[44]](#cite_note-51)

### Republic era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Republic era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Shanghai_Bund_seen_from_the_French_Concession.jpg/250px-Shanghai_Bund_seen_from_the_French_Concession.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_Bund_seen_from_the_French_Concession.jpg)

[The Bund](/wiki/The_Bund "The Bund") as seen from the [French Concession](/wiki/Shanghai_French_Concession "Shanghai French Concession") in the 1920s

In 1912, the Old City walls were dismantled as they blocked the city's expansion.[[45]](#cite_note-52) In July 1921, the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") was founded in the [Shanghai French Concession](/wiki/Shanghai_French_Concession "Shanghai French Concession").[[40]](#cite_note-SHChronicles-47) On 30 May 1925, the [May Thirtieth Movement](/wiki/May_Thirtieth_Movement "May Thirtieth Movement") broke out when a worker in a Japanese-owned [cotton mill](/wiki/Cotton_mill "Cotton mill") was shot and killed by a Japanese foreman.[[46]](#cite_note-Ku-53) Workers in the city then launched [general strikes](/wiki/General_strikes "General strikes") against [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism"), which became nationwide protests that gave rise to [Chinese nationalism](/wiki/Chinese_nationalism "Chinese nationalism").[[47]](#cite_note-54)

The golden age of Shanghai began with its elevation to municipality after it was separated from [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") on 7 July 1927.[[40]](#cite_note-SHChronicles-47)[[48]](#cite_note-GovHistory-55) This new Chinese municipality was 494.69 km2 (191.0 sq mi), and included the districts of [Baoshan](/wiki/Baoshan_District,_Shanghai "Baoshan District, Shanghai"), [Yangpu](/wiki/Yangpu_District "Yangpu District"), [Zhabei](/wiki/Zhabei "Zhabei"), [Nanshi](/wiki/Nanshi_District,_Shanghai "Nanshi District, Shanghai"), and [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong "Pudong").[[48]](#cite_note-GovHistory-55) Headed by a Chinese mayor and municipal council, the city's government implemented the [Greater Shanghai Plan](/wiki/Greater_Shanghai_Plan "Greater Shanghai Plan") to create a new city center in Jiangwan town of Yangpu district, outside the boundaries of the foreign concessions.[[49]](#cite_note-56) The city became a commercial and financial hub of the [Asia-Pacific](/wiki/Asia-Pacific "Asia-Pacific") region in the 1930s.[[50]](#cite_note-1930hub-57) During the ensuing decades, citizens of many countries immigrated to Shanghai; those who stayed for long periods⁠⁠ called themselves "[Shanghailanders](/wiki/Shanghailander "Shanghailander")".[[51]](#cite_note-58) In the 1920s and 1930s, almost 20,000 [White Russians](/wiki/White_Movement "White Movement") fled the newly established [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") to reside in Shanghai.[[52]](#cite_note-59) These [Shanghai Russians](/wiki/Shanghai_Russians "Shanghai Russians") constituted the second-largest foreign community. By 1932, Shanghai had become the world's fifth-largest city and home to 70,000 foreigners.[[53]](#cite_note-60) In the 1930s, approximately 30,000 Jewish refugees from Europe arrived in the city.[[54]](#cite_note-61)

#### Japanese invasion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Japanese invasion")]

Main article: [Battle of Shanghai](/wiki/Battle_of_Shanghai "Battle of Shanghai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Shanghai1937city_zhabei_fire.jpg/250px-Shanghai1937city_zhabei_fire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai1937city_zhabei_fire.jpg)

[Zhabei District](/wiki/Zhabei_District "Zhabei District") on fire, 1937

On [28 January 1932](/wiki/January_28_incident "January 28 incident"), Japanese military forces invaded Shanghai. More than 10,000 shops and hundreds of factories and public buildings[[55]](#cite_note-62) were destroyed, leaving Zhabei district ruined. About 18,000 civilians were either killed, injured, or declared missing.[[40]](#cite_note-SHChronicles-47) A ceasefire was brokered on 5 May.[[56]](#cite_note-63) In 1937, the [Battle of Shanghai](/wiki/Battle_of_Shanghai "Battle of Shanghai") resulted in the occupation of the Chinese-administered parts of Shanghai outside of the International Settlement and the French Concession. People who stayed in the occupied city experienced hunger, oppression, or death.[[57]](#cite_note-64) The foreign concessions were occupied by the Japanese on 8 December 1941 and remained occupied until Japan's surrender in 1945.[[58]](#cite_note-65)

Many Jewish people arrived in Shanghai during the Japanese occupation period. A vice-consul for Japan in Lithuania, [Chiune Sugihara](/wiki/Chiune_Sugihara "Chiune Sugihara"), issued thousands of visas to Jewish refugees escaping the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust"), and the Japanese government transferred many of them to Shanghai by November 1941. Other Jewish refugees traveled from Italy. The refugees from Europe were interned in the [Shanghai Ghetto](/wiki/Shanghai_Ghetto "Shanghai Ghetto") in Hongkou District after the Japanese [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor"). After the [surrender of Japan](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan"), the Chinese Army liberated the Ghetto, and most of the Jews left over the next few years.[[59]](#cite_note-66)

### People's Republic era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: People's Republic era")]

On 27 May 1949, the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") took control of Shanghai through the [Shanghai Campaign](/wiki/Shanghai_Campaign "Shanghai Campaign"). Under the new People's Republic of China (PRC), Shanghai was one of only three municipalities not merged into neighboring provinces (the others being [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") and [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin")).[[60]](#cite_note-67) Most foreign firms moved their offices from Shanghai to [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), as part of a foreign [divestment](/wiki/Divestment "Divestment") due to the PRC's victory.[[61]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/1967-12_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E5%B8%82%E5%8D%97%E4%BA%AC%E8%B7%AF.jpg/250px-1967-12_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E5%B8%82%E5%8D%97%E4%BA%AC%E8%B7%AF.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967-12_1967%E5%B9%B4_%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E5%B8%82%E5%8D%97%E4%BA%AC%E8%B7%AF.jpg)

[Nanjing Road](/wiki/Nanjing_Road "Nanjing Road"), 1967, during the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution")

After the war, Shanghai's economy was restored. From 1949 to 1952, the city's agricultural and industrial output increased by 51.5% and 94.2%, respectively.[[40]](#cite_note-SHChronicles-47) As the industrial center of China with the most skilled industrial workers, Shanghai became a center for radical [leftism](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") during the 1950s and 1960s.[[62]](#cite_note-69) During the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") (1966–1976), Shanghai's society was severely damaged. The majority of the workers in the Shanghai branch of the [People's Bank of China](/wiki/People%27s_Bank_of_China "People's Bank of China") were [Red Guards](/wiki/Red_Guards "Red Guards"), and they formed a group called the Anti-Economy Liaison Headquarters within the branch.[[63]](#cite_note-Liu-2023-70): 38 The Anti-Economy Liaison Headquarters dismantled economic organizations in Shanghai, investigated bank withdrawals, and disrupted regular bank service in the city.[[63]](#cite_note-Liu-2023-70): 38 Despite the disruptions of the Cultural Revolution, Shanghai maintained economic production with a positive annual growth rate.[[40]](#cite_note-SHChronicles-47)

In 1990, [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") permitted Shanghai to initiate economic reforms, which reintroduced foreign capital to the city and developed the Pudong district, resulting in the birth of [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui "Lujiazui").[[64]](#cite_note-71) That year, the China's central government designated Shanghai as the "Dragon Head" of the [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up").[[65]](#cite_note-72) In 2022, Shanghai experienced a large outbreak of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19") cases and the [Chinese government locked down](/wiki/2022_Shanghai_COVID-19_outbreak "2022 Shanghai COVID-19 outbreak") the entire city on 5 April. This resulted in widespread food shortages across the city as food-supply chains were severely disrupted. These restrictions were lifted on 1 June.[[66]](#cite_note-73)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Shanghai](/wiki/Geography_of_Shanghai "Geography of Shanghai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Sprawling_Shanghai_2016-07-20.jpg/250px-Sprawling_Shanghai_2016-07-20.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sprawling_Shanghai_2016-07-20.jpg)

The urban area of Shanghai in 2016, along with [its major islands](/wiki/Islands_of_Shanghai "Islands of Shanghai"). From northwest to southeast: [Chongming](/wiki/Chongming_Island "Chongming Island"), [Changxing](/wiki/Changxing_Island_(Shanghai) "Changxing Island (Shanghai)"), [Hengsha](/wiki/Hengsha_Island "Hengsha Island"), and the [Jiuduansha](/wiki/Jiuduansha "Jiuduansha") shoals off Pudong. The Yangtze's natural [sediment discharge](/wiki/Sediment_transport "Sediment transport") can be seen.

Shanghai is located on the Yangtze [Estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") of China's east coast, with the Yangtze River to the north, [Hangzhou Bay](/wiki/Hangzhou_Bay "Hangzhou Bay") to the south, and the [East China Sea](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea") to the east. The land is formed by the Yangtze's natural [deposition](/wiki/Deposition_(geology) "Deposition (geology)") and modern [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") projects. It has [sandy soil](/wiki/Loam "Loam"), and skyscrapers have to be built with deep concrete piles to avoid sinking into the soft ground.[[67]](#cite_note-SHChroniclesEnvir-74) The provincial-level [Municipality of Shanghai](/wiki/Municipality_of_Shanghai "Municipality of Shanghai") administers the estuary and many of its [surrounding islands](/wiki/Islands_of_Shanghai "Islands of Shanghai"). It borders the provinces of [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") to the south and [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu") to the west and north.[[68]](#cite_note-location-75) The municipality's northernmost point is on [Chongming Island](/wiki/Chongming_Island "Chongming Island"), the second-largest [island in mainland China](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_China "List of islands of China") after its expansion during the 20th century.[[69]](#cite_note-76)

Shanghai is located on an [alluvial plain](/wiki/Alluvial_plain "Alluvial plain") and the vast majority of its 6,340.5-square-kilometre (2,448.1-square-mile) land area is flat, with an average elevation of four metres (13 feet).[[7]](#cite_note-topo-7) Tidal flat ecosystems exist around the estuary, but they have been reclaimed for agricultural purposes.[[70]](#cite_note-77) The city's few hills, such as [She Shan](/wiki/Sheshan_Hill "Sheshan Hill"), lie to the southwest; its highest point is the peak of [Dajinshan Island](/wiki/Dajinshan_Island "Dajinshan Island") (103 metres or 338 feet) in Hangzhou Bay.[[7]](#cite_note-topo-7) Shanghai has rivers, canals, streams, and lakes, and it is known for its rich water resources as part of the [Lake Tai](/wiki/Lake_Tai "Lake Tai") [drainage basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin").[[71]](#cite_note-waterresources-78)

Downtown Shanghai is bisected by the Huangpu River, a man-made [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of the Yangtze created by order of Lord Chunshen during the Warring States period.[[30]](#cite_note-ancient-35) The historic center of the city was located on the west bank of the Huangpu ([Puxi](/wiki/Puxi "Puxi")), near the mouth of Suzhou Creek, connecting it with Lake Tai and the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_of_China "Grand Canal of China"). The central financial district, Lujiazui, was established on the east bank of the Huangpu (Pudong). Along Shanghai's eastern shore, the destruction of local [wetlands](/wiki/Wetlands "Wetlands") due to the construction of [Pudong International Airport](/wiki/Pudong_International_Airport "Pudong International Airport") has been partially offset by the protection and expansion of a nearby shoal, [Jiuduansha](/wiki/Jiuduansha "Jiuduansha"), as a nature preserve.[[72]](#cite_note-79)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Shanghai has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfa*), with an average annual temperature of 17.5 °C (63.5 °F) for downtown areas and 16.2–17.2 °C (61.2–63.0 °F) for suburbs.[[67]](#cite_note-SHChroniclesEnvir-74) The city experiences four distinct seasons. Winters are temperate to cold and damp—northwesterly winds from [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") can cause nighttime temperatures to drop below freezing. Each year, there are an average of 4.7 days with snowfall and 1.6 days with snow cover.[[67]](#cite_note-SHChroniclesEnvir-74) Summers are hot and humid, and occasional downpours or [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorms "Thunderstorms") can be expected. On average, 14.5 days exceed 35 °C (95 °F) annually. In summer and the beginning of autumn, the city is susceptible to [typhoons](/wiki/Typhoon "Typhoon").[[73]](#cite_note-wiphatelegraph-80)

The most pleasant seasons are generally spring, although changeable and often rainy, and autumn, which is usually sunny and dry. With monthly percent possible sunshine ranging from 28% in June to 46% in August, the city receives 1,754 hours of bright sunshine annually.[[h]](#cite_note-81) According to China's seasonal division standard, from 2001 to 2025, Shanghai enters spring on 9 March, summer on 15 May, autumn on 5 October, and winter on 4 December. The average temperature for the three weeks from 19 July to 8 August is above 30 °C (86.0 °F). Extremes since 1951 have ranged from −10.1 °C (13.8 °F) on 31 January 1977 (unofficial record of −12.1 °C (10.2 °F) was set on 19 January 1893) to 40.9 °C (105.6 °F) on 21 July 2017[[74]](#cite_note-82) and 13 July 2022[[75]](#cite_note-83) at a [weather station](/wiki/Weather_station "Weather station") in [Xujiahui](/wiki/Xujiahui "Xujiahui"). It also has 32.1 °C (89.8 °F) as the highest ever daily minimum temperature at Xujiahui on 2 August 2024.[[76]](#cite_note-84)
In 2025, the average temperature of Xujiahui was 19.4°C, which was 1.9°C higher than the average from 1991 to 2020.

## Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

[The Bund](/wiki/The_Bund "The Bund"), located by the bank of the Huangpu River, is home to a row of early 20th-century architecture, ranging in style from the [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") [HSBC Building](/wiki/HSBC_Building,_Shanghai "HSBC Building, Shanghai") to the [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") Sassoon House (now part of the [Peace Hotel](/wiki/Peace_Hotel "Peace Hotel")).[[82]](#cite_note-art_deco-90) The area has been revitalized several times: the first was in 1986, with a new [promenade](/wiki/Esplanade "Esplanade") by the Dutch architect Paulus Snoeren.[[83]](#cite_note-Potter-91) The second was before the [2010 Expo](/wiki/Expo_2010 "Expo 2010"), which includes restoration of the century-old [Waibaidu Bridge](/wiki/Waibaidu_Bridge "Waibaidu Bridge") and reconfiguration of traffic flow.[[84]](#cite_note-92)

Shanghai's construction boom during the 1920s and 1930s caused the city to have several Art Deco buildings.[[82]](#cite_note-art_deco-90) [László Hudec](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Hudec "László Hudec"), a [Hungarian-Slovak](/wiki/Hungarians_in_Slovakia "Hungarians in Slovakia") who lived in the city between 1918 and 1947,[[85]](#cite_note-93) designed Art Deco buildings such as the [Park Hotel](/wiki/Park_Hotel_Shanghai "Park Hotel Shanghai"), the [Grand Cinema](/wiki/Grand_Cinema_(Shanghai) "Grand Cinema (Shanghai)"), and the [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_(Shanghai) "Paramount (Shanghai)").[[86]](#cite_note-94) Other prominent Art Deco-style architects are [Clement Palmer and Arthur Turner](/wiki/P%26T_Group "P&T Group"), who designed the Peace Hotel, the [Metropole Hotel](/w/index.php?title=Metropole_Hotel_(Shanghai)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Metropole Hotel (Shanghai) (page does not exist)"), and the [Broadway Mansions](/wiki/Broadway_Mansions "Broadway Mansions");[[87]](#cite_note-95) and Austrian architect [C.H. Gonda](/wiki/C._H._Gonda "C. H. Gonda"), who designed the [Capitol Theatre](/w/index.php?title=Capitol_Theater_(Shanghai)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Capitol Theater (Shanghai) (page does not exist)").[[83]](#cite_note-Potter-91) One common architectural element is the [shikumen](/wiki/Shikumen "Shikumen") (石库门, "stone storage door") residence, typically two- or three-story gray brick houses with the front yard protected by a heavy wooden door in a stylistic stone arch.[[88]](#cite_note-radical_quaintness-96) Each residence is connected and arranged in straight alleys, known as [longtang](/wiki/Longtang "Longtang")[[i]](#cite_note-98) (弄堂).[[88]](#cite_note-radical_quaintness-96) Shanghai also has [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") neoclassical architecture or [Stalinist architecture](/wiki/Stalinist_architecture "Stalinist architecture"): most were erected between the founding of the [People's Republic](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") in 1949 and the [Sino-Soviet Split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_Split "Sino-Soviet Split") in the late 1960s when Soviet personnel came to China to aid in the development of a communist state. An example of Soviet neoclassical architecture in Shanghai is the [Shanghai Exhibition Center](/wiki/Shanghai_Exhibition_Center "Shanghai Exhibition Center").[[90]](#cite_note-Lonely_Planet_-_undated_-_Lonely_Planet_review_for_Shanghai_Exhibition_Center-99)

Shanghai has [*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] making it the fifth [city in the world with the most skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_cities_with_the_most_skyscrapers "List of cities with the most skyscrapers").[[91]](#cite_note-100) Some of [Shanghai's skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_skyscrapers_in_Shanghai "List of skyscrapers in Shanghai") include the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower "Jin Mao Tower"), the [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center "Shanghai World Financial Center"), and the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower "Shanghai Tower"), which was completed in 2015 and is currently the tallest building in China and the [third tallest in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings "List of tallest buildings").[[92]](#cite_note-101) The Oriental Pearl Tower, at 468 m (1,535 ft), is located nearby at the northern tip of Lujiazui.[[93]](#cite_note-102) Many areas in the former foreign concessions are well-preserved.[[82]](#cite_note-art_deco-90) Despite rampant redevelopment, the Old City retains traditional architecture and designs, such as the [Yu Garden](/wiki/Yu_Garden "Yu Garden"), an elaborate [Jiangnan](/wiki/Jiangnan "Jiangnan") style garden.[[94]](#cite_note-103)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Politics")]

Main articles: [Politics of Shanghai](/wiki/Politics_of_Shanghai "Politics of Shanghai"), [Shanghai Municipal Committee of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Shanghai Municipal Committee of the Chinese Communist Party"), and [Shanghai Municipal People's Government](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_People%27s_Government "Shanghai Municipal People's Government")

### Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Shanghai_Government_Building.jpg/250px-Shanghai_Government_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_Government_Building.jpg)

Shanghai Municipal Government building

Like all [governing institutions in mainland China](/wiki/Politics_of_China "Politics of China"), Shanghai has a parallel party-government system,[[99]](#cite_note-PoliticalSystem-108) in which the [CCP Committee Secretary](/wiki/Party_Secretary_of_Shanghai "Party Secretary of Shanghai"), officially termed the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") Shanghai Municipal Committee Secretary, outranks the [Mayor](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Shanghai "List of mayors of Shanghai").[[100]](#cite_note-109) The [CCP committee](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Shanghai Municipal Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") acts as the top policy-formulation body, and typically composed of 12 members (including the secretary); it has control over the [Shanghai Municipal People's Government](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_People%27s_Government "Shanghai Municipal People's Government").[[101]](#cite_note-110)[[102]](#cite_note-111)

Political power in Shanghai has been a stepping stone to higher positions in the central government. Since [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin") became the [General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") in June 1989, several former Shanghai party secretaries and deputy party secretaries were elevated to the [Politburo Standing Committee](/wiki/Politburo_Standing_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo Standing Committee of the Chinese Communist Party"), the *de facto* highest decision-making body in China.[[99]](#cite_note-PoliticalSystem-108) Officials with ties to the Shanghai administration collectively form a powerful faction in the central government known as the [Shanghai Clique](/wiki/Shanghai_Clique "Shanghai Clique"), which has often been viewed as competing against the rival [Youth League Faction](/wiki/Youth_League_Faction "Youth League Faction") over personnel appointments and policy decisions.[[103]](#cite_note-112)

### Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

Main articles: [List of administrative divisions of Shanghai](/wiki/List_of_administrative_divisions_of_Shanghai "List of administrative divisions of Shanghai") and [List of township-level divisions of Shanghai](/wiki/List_of_township-level_divisions_of_Shanghai "List of township-level divisions of Shanghai")

Shanghai is one of the four [municipalities](/wiki/Direct-administered_municipalities_of_China "Direct-administered municipalities of China") under the direct administration of the [Central People's Government](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "State Council of the People's Republic of China"),[[104]](#cite_note-113) and is divided into 16 [districts](/wiki/District_of_China "District of China"). These are further divided to 108 [subdistricts](/wiki/Subdistrict_(China) "Subdistrict (China)"), 106 [towns](/wiki/Town_(China) "Town (China)") and 2 [townships](/wiki/Township_(China) "Township (China)").[[105]](#cite_note-114)

When the [Shanghai Municipal People's Government](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_People%27s_Government "Shanghai Municipal People's Government") was founded in 1949, the land area governed was 663.5 square kilometres (256.2 sq mi), largely located within the present-day [Outer Ring Expressway](/wiki/Shanghai_Outer_Ring_Expressway "Shanghai Outer Ring Expressway").[[106]](#cite_note-115) In 1958, ten [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_China "Counties of China") were reassigned under Shanghai from [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu").[[107]](#cite_note-116)[[108]](#cite_note-117) District reorganizations saw several counties in the suburbs become districts between 1988 and 2015, and Chongming was the last county to be retitled as a district in 2015.[[109]](#cite_note-118)

Shanghai also administers several [enclaves](/wiki/Enclaves "Enclaves") in Jiangsu and [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui") provinces.[[110]](#cite_note-119) Local residents hold Shanghai *[household registration](/wiki/Hukou "Hukou")* and enjoy benefits identical to Shanghai residents.[[111]](#cite_note-120)

| **Greater Shanghai Metropolitan Area** | **53,407** | **79,634,907** | **CN¥ 12.983 trillion** | **US$1.927 trillion** |

The city is a global center for [finance](/wiki/Finance "Finance") and [innovation](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_China "Science and technology in China"),[[116]](#cite_note-125)[[117]](#cite_note-126) and a national center for commerce, trade, and transportation,[[118]](#cite_note-127) with the world's busiest container port—the [Port of Shanghai](/wiki/Port_of_Shanghai "Port of Shanghai").[[119]](#cite_note-128) As of 2022, the Greater Shanghai metropolitan area, which includes [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou "Suzhou"), [Wuxi](/wiki/Wuxi "Wuxi"), [Nantong](/wiki/Nantong "Nantong"), [Ningbo](/wiki/Ningbo "Ningbo"), [Jiaxing](/wiki/Jiaxing "Jiaxing"), [Zhoushan](/wiki/Zhoushan "Zhoushan"), and [Huzhou](/wiki/Huzhou "Huzhou"), was estimated to produce a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") ([nominal](/wiki/GDP_(nominal) "GDP (nominal)")) of nearly 13 trillion [RMB](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") ($1.9 trillion).[[14]](#cite_note-GDP2022-15) As of 2020, the economy of Shanghai was estimated to be $1 trillion ([PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")), ranking the most productive metro area of China and among the top ten [largest metropolitan economies](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP") in the world.[[120]](#cite_note-129) Shanghai's six largest industries—[retail](/wiki/Retail "Retail"), finance, [IT](/wiki/IT "IT"), [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate"), [machine manufacturing](/wiki/Machine_industry "Machine industry"), and [automotive manufacturing](/wiki/Automotive_manufacturing "Automotive manufacturing")—comprise about half the city's GDP.[[121]](#cite_note-130)

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), Shanghai had a GDP of [CN¥](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi")5.67 trillion ($814 billion in nominal; $1.66 trillion in [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")) that makes up 4% of China's GDP, and a [GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_provincial-level_divisions_by_GDP_per_capita "List of Chinese provincial-level divisions by GDP per capita") of CN¥228,280 (US$32,840 in nominal; US$66,944 in PPP).[[122]](#cite_note-131)[[123]](#cite_note-132) In 2022, the average annual [disposable income](/wiki/Disposable_income "Disposable income") of Shanghai's residents was CN¥79,610 (US$11,836) per capita, while the average annual salary of people employed in urban units in Shanghai was CN¥212,476 (US$31,589),[[124]](#cite_note-data2022-133) making it one of the wealthiest cities in China,[[125]](#cite_note-134) but also the most expensive city in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") to live in according to a 2023 study by the [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit").[[126]](#cite_note-135) According to Julius Baer's Global Wealth and Lifestyle Report, Shanghai was the most expensive city in the world for living a luxurious lifestyle in 2021.[[127]](#cite_note-136)

In 2023, the city's imports and exports reached CN¥7.73 trillion (US$1.07 trillion), accounting for 18.5% of the national total.[[128]](#cite_note-137) In 2022, Shanghai was ranked [fifth-highest](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_number_of_billionaires "List of cities by number of billionaires") in the number of billionaires by Forbes.[[129]](#cite_note-138) Shanghai's [nominal GDP](/wiki/Nominal_GDP "Nominal GDP") was projected to reach US$1.3 trillion in 2035 (ranking first in China), making it one of the world's top 5 major cities in terms of [GRP](/wiki/Gross_regional_product "Gross regional product") according to a study by Oxford Economics.[[130]](#cite_note-139) As of August 2024, Shanghai ranked 4th in the world and 2nd in [Greater China](/wiki/Greater_China "Greater China") (after Beijing) by the largest number of the [*Fortune* Global 500](/wiki/Fortune_Global_500#Fortune_Global_500_of_2021 "Fortune Global 500") companies.[[15]](#cite_note-FG-16)

In the last two decades, Shanghai has been one of the fastest-developing cities in the world; it has recorded double-digit GDP growth in almost every year between 1992 and 2008, before the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[138]](#cite_note-147)

### Finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Finance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Shanghai_Stock_Exchange_2.jpg/250px-Shanghai_Stock_Exchange_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_Stock_Exchange_2.jpg)

The [Shanghai Stock Exchange](/wiki/Shanghai_Stock_Exchange "Shanghai Stock Exchange") is one of the largest stock exchanges in the world by market capitalization.

Shanghai is a global [financial center](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center"), ranking third in Asia and eighth globally on the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").[[139]](#cite_note-148) Shanghai is also a large hub of the Chinese and global technology industry and home to a large startup ecosystem. As of 2021, the city was ranked as the 2nd [Fintech](/wiki/Fintech "Fintech") powerhouse in the world after New York City.[[140]](#cite_note-149)

As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), the [Shanghai Stock Exchange](/wiki/Shanghai_Stock_Exchange "Shanghai Stock Exchange") had a [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of US$4.02 trillion, making it the largest [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange") in China and the [fourth-largest stock exchange](/wiki/List_of_stock_exchanges "List of stock exchanges") in the world.[[141]](#cite_note-150) In 2009, the trading volume of six key commodities—including rubber, copper, and zinc—on the [Shanghai Futures Exchange](/wiki/Shanghai_Futures_Exchange "Shanghai Futures Exchange") all ranked first globally.[[142]](#cite_note-151) By the end of 2017, Shanghai had 1,491 financial institutions, of which 251 were foreign-invested.[[143]](#cite_note-hktdc.com-152)

### Manufacturing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Manufacturing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/F-22P_PNS_Zulfiquar.JPG/250px-F-22P_PNS_Zulfiquar.JPG)](/wiki/File:F-22P_PNS_Zulfiquar.JPG)

The [F-22P](/wiki/F-22P "F-22P") frigate built by [Hudong-Zhonghua](/wiki/Hudong%E2%80%93Zhonghua_Shipbuilding "Hudong–Zhonghua Shipbuilding") for the [Pakistan Navy](/wiki/Pakistan_Navy "Pakistan Navy")

As one of the main industrial centers of China, Shanghai plays a key role in domestic manufacturing and [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry"). Several industrial zones—including Shanghai Hongqiao Economic and Technological Development Zone, Jinqiao Export Economic Processing Zone, Minhang Economic and Technological Development Zone, and Shanghai Caohejing High-Tech Development Zone—are backbones of Shanghai's [secondary sector](/wiki/Secondary_sector "Secondary sector"). Shanghai is home to China's largest steelmaker [Baosteel Group](/wiki/Baosteel_Group "Baosteel Group"), China's largest [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding") base [Hudong–Zhonghua Shipbuilding Group](/wiki/Hudong%E2%80%93Zhonghua_Shipbuilding "Hudong–Zhonghua Shipbuilding"), and one of China's oldest shipbuilders, the [Jiangnan Shipyard](/wiki/Jiangnan_Shipyard "Jiangnan Shipyard").[[144]](#cite_note-153)[[145]](#cite_note-154) In auto manufacturing, the Shanghai-based [SAIC Motor](/wiki/SAIC_Motor "SAIC Motor") is one of the three largest automotive corporations in China, and has strategic partnerships with [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen") and [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors").[[146]](#cite_note-155) The company ranked 84 on the Fortune Global 500 list in 2023.[[147]](#cite_note-156)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Tourism")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of tourist attractions in Shanghai](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_Shanghai "List of tourist attractions in Shanghai").

In 2017, the number of domestic tourists to the city increased by 7.5% to 318 million, while the number of overseas tourists increased by 2.2% to 8.73 million.[[143]](#cite_note-hktdc.com-152) In 2017, Shanghai was the highest earning [tourist city](/wiki/Tourist_city "Tourist city") in the world.[[148]](#cite_note-157) According to the [International Congress and Convention Association](/wiki/International_Congress_and_Convention_Association "International Congress and Convention Association"), Shanghai hosted 82 international meetings in 2018, a 34% increase from 61 in 2017.[[149]](#cite_note-158)[[150]](#cite_note-159) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), it had 57 [five-star hotels](/wiki/Five_star_hotel "Five star hotel"), 52 [four star hotels](/wiki/Four_star_hotel "Four star hotel"), 1,942 [travel agencies](/wiki/Travel_agencies "Travel agencies"), 144 [rated tourist attractions](/wiki/Tourist_Attraction_Rating_Categories_of_China "Tourist Attraction Rating Categories of China"), and 34 [red tourist](/wiki/Red_tourism "Red tourism") attractions.[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141) In 2023, Shanghai had 3.64 million tourists, a 4.8-fold growth compared to 2022. It generated CN¥177.12 billion (US$24.53 billion) in value, a 98.5% increase from the previous year. The number of foreign tourists reached 2.41 million, with a 5.2-fold increase.[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141)

### Free-trade zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Free-trade zone")]

In September 2013, the city launched the [Shanghai Free-Trade Zone](/wiki/Shanghai_Free-Trade_Zone "Shanghai Free-Trade Zone")—the first [free-trade zone](/wiki/Free-trade_zone "Free-trade zone") in mainland China. It introduced several reforms to incentivize foreign investment. *[The Banker](/wiki/The_Banker "The Banker")* reported that Shanghai attracted the highest volumes of financial sector [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") in the Asia-Pacific region in 2013.[[151]](#cite_note-160)[[152]](#cite_note-161) As of October 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), it is the second largest free-trade zone in mainland China in terms of land area (behind [Hainan Free Trade Zone](/w/index.php?title=Hainan_Free_Trade_Zone&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hainan Free Trade Zone (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%EF%BC%88%E6%B5%B7%E5%8D%97%EF%BC%89%E8%87%AA%E7%94%B1%E8%B4%B8%E6%98%93%E8%AF%95%E9%AA%8C%E5%8C%BA "zh:中国（海南）自由贸易试验区")][[153]](#cite_note-162)) covering an area of 240.22 km2 (92.75 sq mi) and integrating four existing bonded zones—Waigaoqiao Free Trade Zone, Waigaoqiao Free Trade Logistics Park, Yangshan Free Trade Port Area, and Pudong Airport Comprehensive Free Trade Zone.[[154]](#cite_note-163)[[155]](#cite_note-164) Commodities entering the zone are exempt from duty and customs clearance.[[156]](#cite_note-165)

Main article: [Demographics of Shanghai](/wiki/Demographics_of_Shanghai "Demographics of Shanghai")

As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), Shanghai had a population of 24,874,500, including 14,801,700 (59.5%) [hukou](/wiki/Hukou "Hukou") holders (registered locally).[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), 89.3% of Shanghai's population lives in urban areas, and 10.7% live in rural areas.[[124]](#cite_note-data2022-133) Based on the population of its total administrative area, Shanghai is the second largest of the four municipalities of China, behind [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), but is generally considered the largest Chinese city because the urban population of Chongqing is much smaller.[[158]](#cite_note-chan_paper-167) According to the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), Shanghai's [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") has an estimated population of 34 million.[[159]](#cite_note-OECD-168)

According to the Shanghai Municipal Statistics Bureau, about 157,900 residents in Shanghai are foreigners, including 28,900 Japanese, 21,900 Americans, and 20,800 Koreans.[[160]](#cite_note-SHSTAT2018:2.11-169) The actual number of foreign citizens in the city is probably much higher.[[161]](#cite_note-170) Shanghai is also a domestic immigration city—40.3% (9.8 million) of the city's residents are from other regions of China.[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141)

Shanghai has a [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") of 83.18 years for the city's registered population,[[162]](#cite_note-171) the highest life expectancy of [all cities in mainland China](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_China_by_life_expectancy "List of cities in China by life expectancy"). This has also caused the city to [experience population aging](/wiki/Aging_of_China "Aging of China")—in 2021, 17.4% (4.3 million) of the city's registered population was aged 65 or above.[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141) In 2017, the Chinese government implemented population controls for Shanghai, resulting in a population decline of 10,000 people by the end of the year.[[163]](#cite_note-172)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Religion")]

See also: [Religion in China](/wiki/Religion_in_China "Religion in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Shanghai%2C_China_%28Unsplash_8T9p4FDu590%29.jpg/250px-Shanghai%2C_China_%28Unsplash_8T9p4FDu590%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai,_China_(Unsplash_8T9p4FDu590).jpg)

Bird's-eye view of the golden [pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda") of [Jing'an Temple](/wiki/Jing%27an_Temple "Jing'an Temple")

Due to its cosmopolitan history, Shanghai has a blend of religious heritage; religious buildings and institutions are scattered around the city. According to a 2012 survey, 13.1% of the city's population belongs to organized religions, including [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhists "Buddhists") with 10.4%, [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") with 1.9%, [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics") with 0.7%, and other faiths with 0.1%. The remaining 86.9% of the population could be either [atheists](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist") or involved in [worship of nature deities and ancestors](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") or [folk religious sects](/wiki/Chinese_salvationist_religions "Chinese salvationist religions").[[164]](#cite_note-CFPS2012-173)

Religion in Shanghai (2012):

1. Chinese folk religion, or atheist (87.5%)
2. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (10.3%)
3. [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (1.88%)
4. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (0.36%)

Buddhism, in its [Chinese varieties](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"), has had a presence in Shanghai since the [Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms "Three Kingdoms") period, during which the [Longhua Temple](/wiki/Longhua_Temple "Longhua Temple")—the largest temple in Shanghai—and the [Jing'an Temple](/wiki/Jing%27an_Temple "Jing'an Temple") were founded.[[165]](#cite_note-Buddhism-174) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), Buddhism in Shanghai had 114 temples, 1,182 [clergical staff](/wiki/Clergy#Buddhism "Clergy"), and 453,300 registered followers.[[165]](#cite_note-Buddhism-174) The religion also has its own college, the [Shanghai Buddhist College](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Buddhist_College&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shanghai Buddhist College (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E4%BD%9B%E5%AD%A6%E9%99%A2 "zh:上海佛学院")], and its own press, [Shanghai Buddhological Press](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Buddhological_Press&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shanghai Buddhological Press (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E4%BD%9B%E5%AD%A6%E4%B9%A6%E5%B1%80 "zh:上海佛学书局")].[[166]](#cite_note-175)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Photo_of_St._Ignatius_Cathedral%2C_Shanghai_%E5%9C%A3%E4%BE%9D%E7%BA%B3%E7%88%B5%E4%B8%BB%E6%95%99%E5%BA%A7%E5%A0%82.jpg/250px-Photo_of_St._Ignatius_Cathedral%2C_Shanghai_%E5%9C%A3%E4%BE%9D%E7%BA%B3%E7%88%B5%E4%B8%BB%E6%95%99%E5%BA%A7%E5%A0%82.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photo_of_St._Ignatius_Cathedral,_Shanghai_%E5%9C%A3%E4%BE%9D%E7%BA%B3%E7%88%B5%E4%B8%BB%E6%95%99%E5%BA%A7%E5%A0%82.jpg)

The [St. Ignatius Cathedral](/wiki/St._Ignatius_Cathedral "St. Ignatius Cathedral")

Catholicism was brought into Shanghai in 1608 by Italian missionary [Lazzaro Cattaneo](/wiki/Lazzaro_Cattaneo "Lazzaro Cattaneo").[[167]](#cite_note-176) The [Apostolic Vicariate](/wiki/Apostolic_Vicariate "Apostolic Vicariate") of Shanghai was erected in 1933, and was further elevated to the [Diocese of Shanghai](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Shanghai "Roman Catholic Diocese of Shanghai") in 1946.[[168]](#cite_note-177) The [St. Ignatius Cathedral](/wiki/St._Ignatius_Cathedral "St. Ignatius Cathedral") in Xujiahui is the largest Catholic church in the city.[[169]](#cite_note-178) Shanghai has the highest concentration of urban [Catholics in China](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_China "Catholic Church in China").[[170]](#cite_note-179): 38 Other forms of [Christianity in Shanghai](/wiki/Christianity_in_Shanghai "Christianity in Shanghai") include [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") minorities and, since 1996, registered Christian Protestant churches. The Protestant [All Saints Church](/wiki/All_Saints_Church,_Shanghai "All Saints Church, Shanghai") in [Huangpu](/wiki/Huangpu,_Shanghai "Huangpu, Shanghai") was built in 1925 and features a [Neo-Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival_architecture "Romanesque Revival architecture") tower.[[171]](#cite_note-180)

Prominent Jewish families immigrated to Shanghai when the [Treaty of Nanking](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking") opened the city to Western populations.[[172]](#cite_note-ShanghaiJews-181) During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), thousands of Jews emigrated to Shanghai to flee [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"). They lived in a designated area called the Shanghai Ghetto and formed a community centered on the Ohel Moishe Synagogue, (now the [Shanghai Jewish Refugees Museum](/wiki/Shanghai_Jewish_Refugees_Museum "Shanghai Jewish Refugees Museum")).[[173]](#cite_note-182) In 1939, [Horace Kadoorie](/wiki/Horace_Kadoorie "Horace Kadoorie"), the head of the powerful philanthropic Sephardic Jewish family in Shanghai, founded the Shanghai Jewish Youth Association to support Jewish refugees through English education so they would be prepared to emigrate from Shanghai.[[174]](#cite_note-183)

[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") came into Shanghai during the Yuan dynasty. The city's first mosque, [Songjiang Mosque](/wiki/Songjiang_Mosque "Songjiang Mosque"), was built during the Zhizheng (至正) era under [Emperor Huizong](/wiki/Toghon_Tem%C3%BCr "Toghon Temür") (reigned 1333 – 1368). Shanghai's [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") population increased in the 19th and early 20th centuries (when the city was a treaty port), during which time many mosques—including the [Xiaotaoyuan Mosque](/wiki/Xiaotaoyuan_Mosque "Xiaotaoyuan Mosque"), the [Huxi Mosque](/wiki/Huxi_Mosque "Huxi Mosque"), and the [Pudong Mosque](/wiki/Pudong_Mosque "Pudong Mosque")—were built. The Shanghai Islamic Association is located in the Xiaotaoyuan Mosque in Huangpu.[[175]](#cite_note-184) According to the 2010 [census of China](/wiki/Census_in_China "Census in China"), there are an estimated 85,000 Muslims in Shanghai.[[176]](#cite_note-185)

Shanghai has several folk religious temples, including the City God Temple at the heart of the Old City, the [Dajing Ge Pavilion](/wiki/Dajing_Ge_Pavilion "Dajing Ge Pavilion") dedicated to the Three Kingdoms general [Guan Yu](/wiki/Guan_Yu "Guan Yu"), the [Confucian Temple of Shanghai](/wiki/Confucian_Temple_of_Shanghai "Confucian Temple of Shanghai"), and a major Taoist center [Shanghai White Cloud Temple](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_White_Cloud_Temple&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shanghai White Cloud Temple (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B5%B7%E4%B8%8A%E7%99%BD%E4%BA%91%E8%A7%82 "zh:海上白云观")] where the Shanghai Taoist Association locates.[[177]](#cite_note-186)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Language")]

Main article: [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese "Shanghainese")
