The [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") language spoken in the city is Shanghainese, part of the [Taihu Wu](/wiki/Taihu_Wu "Taihu Wu") subgroup of the [Wu Chinese](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") language family. This is different from the national language, [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), which is mutually unintelligible with Wu Chinese.[[179]](#cite_note-188) Modern Shanghainese derives from the indigenous Wu spoken in the former Songjiang prefecture but has been influenced by other dialects of Taihu Wu, most notably [Suzhounese](/wiki/Suzhou_dialect "Suzhou dialect"), and [Ningbonese](/wiki/Ningbo_dialect "Ningbo dialect").[[180]](#cite_note-CZMShanghainese-189)

Before its expansion, the language spoken in Shanghai was not as prominent as those spoken around [Jiaxing](/wiki/Jiaxing "Jiaxing") and later [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou "Suzhou"),[[180]](#cite_note-CZMShanghainese-189) and was known as "the local tongue" (本地閑話), a name which is now used in suburbs only.[[181]](#cite_note-190) In the late 19th century, downtown Shanghainese (市區閑話 or simply 上海閑話) appeared, undergoing rapid changes and replacing Suzhounese as the [prestige dialect](/wiki/Prestige_dialect "Prestige dialect") of the [Yangtze River Delta](/wiki/Yangtze_River_Delta "Yangtze River Delta") region. At the time, most immigration into the city came from the two adjacent provinces, Jiangsu and Zhejiang, the local dialects of which had the greatest influence on Shanghainese. After 1949, Putonghua (Standard Mandarin) also had an impact on Shanghainese because it was promoted by the government.[[180]](#cite_note-CZMShanghainese-189) Since the 1990s, many migrants outside of the Wu-speaking region come to Shanghai for education and jobs; they often cannot speak the local language and use Putonghua (Mandarin) as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca"). Because Putonghua and English were more favored, Shanghainese began to decline, and fluency among young speakers weakened. In recent years, there have been movements within the city to promote the local language and protect it from fading out.[[182]](#cite_note-191)[[183]](#cite_note-192)

## Education and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Education and research")]

Main articles: [Education in Shanghai](/wiki/Education_in_Shanghai "Education in Shanghai"), [Education in China](/wiki/Education_in_China "Education in China"), and [Higher education in China](/wiki/Higher_education_in_China "Higher education in China")

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of universities and colleges in Shanghai](/wiki/List_of_universities_and_colleges_in_Shanghai "List of universities and colleges in Shanghai").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/20191027_Xuhui_Campus_of_SJTU_04.jpg/250px-20191027_Xuhui_Campus_of_SJTU_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:20191027_Xuhui_Campus_of_SJTU_04.jpg)

[Shanghai Jiao Tong University](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiao_Tong_University "Shanghai Jiao Tong University") Library

Shanghai is an international center of research and development and as of 2025, it was ranked [second globally](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_scientific_output "List of cities by scientific output") (after [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing")) by scientific research outputs, as tracked by the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index").[[184]](#cite_note-:0-193) When compared to other countries, Shanghai ranked higher than France and nearly on par with Japan, securing sixth place globally after China, the US, Germany, the United Kingdom, and Japan, according to the Nature Index for 2025. For instance, Shanghai's share of the 2024 Nature Index is 3,153.61, with a count of 6,680, while Japan's share is 3,185.39, with 5,555 counts.[[184]](#cite_note-:0-193)[[185]](#cite_note-194)

As of 2023, Shanghai had 68 universities and colleges, ranking first in [East China](/wiki/East_China "East China") region as a city with most higher education institutions.[[186]](#cite_note-195) The city government's education agency is the [Shanghai Municipal Education Commission](/wiki/Shanghai_Municipal_Education_Commission "Shanghai Municipal Education Commission").[[187]](#cite_note-196)

Shanghai has 15 universities listed in [147 Double First-Class Universities](/wiki/Double_First-Class_Construction "Double First-Class Construction"), ranking second nationwide among Chinese cities (after Beijing). According to the [U.S. News & World Report Best Global University Ranking](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report_Best_Global_Universities_Ranking "U.S. News & World Report Best Global Universities Ranking") for 2025–26, Shanghai had the third highest concentration of universities among all major cities in the world included in the ranking, totaling 22, with three in the top 125 and six in the global top 500.[[188]](#cite_note-197) In the 2025 [Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities"), Shanghai had two in the top 40, three in the top 150 and nine in the top 500.[[189]](#cite_note-198) Some of these universities were selected as "[985 universities](/wiki/Project_985 "Project 985")" or "[211 universities](/wiki/Project_211 "Project 211")" since the 90s by the Chinese government to build world-class universities.[[190]](#cite_note-199)[[191]](#cite_note-200)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Fudan-guanghualou2.jpg/250px-Fudan-guanghualou2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fudan-guanghualou2.jpg)

[Fudan University](/wiki/Fudan_University "Fudan University")

Shanghai has two members ([Fudan University](/wiki/Fudan_University "Fudan University") and [Shanghai Jiao Tong University](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiao_Tong_University "Shanghai Jiao Tong University")) in the [C9 League](/wiki/C9_League "C9 League"), an alliance of elite Chinese universities offering comprehensive and leading education.[[192]](#cite_note-201) These two universities are consistently ranked in the Asia top 10.[[193]](#cite_note-202)[[194]](#cite_note-203) As of 2025, Fudan University and Shanghai Jiao Tong University were ranked in the global top 35 [research comprehensive universities](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") based on aggregate performance from three widely observed university rankings ([THE](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings")+[ARWU](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")+[QS](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")).[[195]](#cite_note-204)

The other two members of [Project 985](/wiki/Project_985 "Project 985"), [Tongji University](/wiki/Tongji_University "Tongji University") and [East China Normal University](/wiki/East_China_Normal_University "East China Normal University"), are also based in Shanghai and internationally; they were ranked they ranked 150–175th globally by the *[Times Higher Education World Reputation Rankings](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_Reputation_Rankings "Times Higher Education World Reputation Rankings")* where .[[196]](#cite_note-205) [Shanghai University of Sport](/wiki/Shanghai_University_of_Sport "Shanghai University of Sport") is also based in the city, which is consistently ranked the best in China among universities specialized in sports,[[197]](#cite_note-206) and as of 2025, SUS was ranked #1 in Asia and #23 globally according to the "Global Ranking of Sport Science Schools and Departments" released by [Shanghai Ranking](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities").[[198]](#cite_note-207)

The city has many [Chinese–foreign joint education institutes](/w/index.php?title=Chinese%E2%80%93foreign_joint_education_institutes&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chinese–foreign joint education institutes (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%A4%96%E5%90%88%E4%BD%9C%E5%8A%9E%E5%AD%A6 "zh:中外合作办学")], such as the [Shanghai University](/wiki/Shanghai_University "Shanghai University")–[University of Technology Sydney](/wiki/University_of_Technology_Sydney "University of Technology Sydney") Business School since 1994, the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan")–Shanghai Jiao Tong University Joint Institute since 2006, and [New York University Shanghai](/wiki/New_York_University_Shanghai "New York University Shanghai")—the first China–U.S. joint venture university—since 2012.[[199]](#cite_note-208)[[200]](#cite_note-209) In 2013, the Shanghai Municipality and the [Chinese Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Chinese_Academy_of_Sciences "Chinese Academy of Sciences") founded the [ShanghaiTech University](/wiki/ShanghaiTech_University "ShanghaiTech University") in the [Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park](/wiki/Zhangjiang_Hi-Tech_Park "Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park") in Pudong.[[201]](#cite_note-210) The city is also a seat of the [Shanghai Academy of Social Sciences](/wiki/Shanghai_Academy_of_Social_Sciences "Shanghai Academy of Social Sciences"), China's oldest [think tank](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank") for the [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities") and [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences").[[202]](#cite_note-211)

By the end of 2023, the city also had a total of 49 institutions for postgraduate education, 900 secondary schools, 70 vocational schools, 664 primary schools, and 31 special education schools. Five years of [primary education](/wiki/Primary_education "Primary education") and four years of [junior secondary](/wiki/Education_in_China#Junior_secondary "Education in China") education are free, with a [gross enrollment ratio](/wiki/Gross_enrollment_ratio "Gross enrollment ratio") of over 99.9%.[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141) In 2009 and 2012, 15-year-old students from Shanghai ranked first in every subject (math, reading, and science) in the [Program for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Program_for_International_Student_Assessment "Program for International Student Assessment").[[203]](#cite_note-212)[[204]](#cite_note-213) The consecutive three-year [senior secondary education](/wiki/Senior_secondary_education "Senior secondary education") is priced and uses the [Senior High School Entrance Examination](/wiki/Senior_High_School_Entrance_Examination "Senior High School Entrance Examination") (*Zhongkao*) as a selection process, with a gross enrollment ratio of 98%.[[205]](#cite_note-214) [Shanghai High School](/wiki/Shanghai_High_School "Shanghai High School"), [No. 2 High School Attached to East China Normal University](/wiki/No._2_High_School_Attached_to_East_China_Normal_University "No. 2 High School Attached to East China Normal University"), [High School Affiliated to Fudan University](/wiki/High_School_Affiliated_to_Fudan_University "High School Affiliated to Fudan University"), and [High School Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University](/wiki/High_School_Affiliated_to_Shanghai_Jiao_Tong_University "High School Affiliated to Shanghai Jiao Tong University")—are termed "The Four Schools" ("四校") of Shanghai and highlighted as having the best teaching quality in the city.[[206]](#cite_note-215)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Shanghai](/wiki/Transport_in_Shanghai "Transport in Shanghai")

### Public

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Public")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Shanghai_Metro_09A04.jpg/250px-Shanghai_Metro_09A04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_Metro_09A04.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Sunwin_SWB6109BEV69G_%28iEV10%29_S0S-0224_and_SWB5129BEV77G_%28iEV12T%29_S5A-0069_at_Zhongshan_Rd_%28S-1%29_Xizang_Rd_S.jpg/250px-Sunwin_SWB6109BEV69G_%28iEV10%29_S0S-0224_and_SWB5129BEV77G_%28iEV12T%29_S5A-0069_at_Zhongshan_Rd_%28S-1%29_Xizang_Rd_S.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sunwin_SWB6109BEV69G_(iEV10)_S0S-0224_and_SWB5129BEV77G_(iEV12T)_S5A-0069_at_Zhongshan_Rd_(S-1)_Xizang_Rd_S.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/A_maglev_train_coming_out%2C_Pudong_International_Airport%2C_Shanghai.jpg/250px-A_maglev_train_coming_out%2C_Pudong_International_Airport%2C_Shanghai.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_maglev_train_coming_out,_Pudong_International_Airport,_Shanghai.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/%E6%9D%BE%E6%B1%9F%E6%9C%89%E8%BD%A8%E7%94%B5%E8%BD%A6_Songjiang_Tram.jpg/120px-%E6%9D%BE%E6%B1%9F%E6%9C%89%E8%BD%A8%E7%94%B5%E8%BD%A6_Songjiang_Tram.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%9D%BE%E6%B1%9F%E6%9C%89%E8%BD%A8%E7%94%B5%E8%BD%A6_Songjiang_Tram.jpg)

Various modes of public transport in Shanghai. From top to bottom, left to right: [Shanghai Metro](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro "Shanghai Metro"), [buses](/wiki/Buses_in_Shanghai "Buses in Shanghai") and [trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybuses_in_Shanghai "Trolleybuses in Shanghai"), [Shanghai Maglev](/wiki/Shanghai_Maglev "Shanghai Maglev") and [Songjiang Tram](/wiki/Songjiang_Tram "Songjiang Tram")

Shanghai has a public transportation system comprising metros, buses, ferries, and taxis, which can be accessed using a [Shanghai Public Transport Card](/wiki/Shanghai_Public_Transport_Card "Shanghai Public Transport Card").[[207]](#cite_note-216)

Shanghai's rapid transit system, the [Shanghai Metro](/wiki/Shanghai_Metro "Shanghai Metro"), incorporates subway and light metro lines and extends to each core urban district as well as neighboring suburban districts. As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), there are 19 metro lines (excluding the [Shanghai maglev train](/wiki/Shanghai_maglev_train "Shanghai maglev train") and [Jinshan railway](/wiki/Jinshan_railway "Jinshan railway")), 508 [stations](/wiki/List_of_Shanghai_Metro_stations "List of Shanghai Metro stations"), and 808 km (502 mi) of lines in operation, making it the [longest network in the world](/wiki/List_of_metro_systems "List of metro systems").[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141) On 8 March 2019, it set the city's daily metro ridership record with 13.3 million.[[208]](#cite_note-ridership_record-217) Opened in 2004, the Shanghai maglev train is the first and the fastest commercial high-speed [maglev](/wiki/Maglev "Maglev") in the world, with a maximum operation speed of 430 km/h (267 mph).[[209]](#cite_note-218)

The first [tram line in Shanghai](/wiki/Trams_in_Shanghai_(1908%E2%80%931975) "Trams in Shanghai (1908–1975)") was opened in 1908. By 1925, there were 328 tramcars and 14 routes operated by Chinese, French, and British companies collaboratively,[[210]](#cite_note-219) all of which were [nationalized](/wiki/Nationalized "Nationalized") in 1949. Since the 1960s, tram lines were either dismantled or replaced by [trolleybus](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus") or [motorbus](/wiki/Motorbus "Motorbus") lines;[[211]](#cite_note-220) the last tram line was demolished in 1975.[[212]](#cite_note-221) Shanghai reintroduced trams in 2010 with the [rubber-tyred](/wiki/Rubber-tyred_tram "Rubber-tyred tram") [Zhangjiang Tram](/wiki/Zhangjiang_Tram "Zhangjiang Tram").[[213]](#cite_note-222) In 2018, the steel wheeled [Songjiang Tram](/wiki/Songjiang_Tram "Songjiang Tram") started operating in Songjiang District.[[214]](#cite_note-223)

Shanghai has the world's most extensive [bus network](/wiki/Buses_in_Shanghai "Buses in Shanghai"), including the world's oldest continuously operating [trolleybus system](/wiki/Trolleybuses_in_Shanghai "Trolleybuses in Shanghai"), with 1,575 lines covering a total length of 8,997 km (5,590 mi) by 2019.[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141) The system is operated by multiple companies.[[215]](#cite_note-224) As of 2024, 30,900 taxis were in operation in Shanghai, which carried 134 million passengers that year.[[216]](#cite_note-225)

### Roads and expressways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Roads and expressways")]

See also: [Expressways of Shanghai](/wiki/Expressways_of_Shanghai "Expressways of Shanghai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Yan%27an_East_Road_Interchange%2C_Shanghai%2C_China_%28Unsplash%29.jpg/250px-Yan%27an_East_Road_Interchange%2C_Shanghai%2C_China_%28Unsplash%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yan%27an_East_Road_Interchange,_Shanghai,_China_(Unsplash).jpg)

[Interchange](/wiki/Interchange_(road) "Interchange (road)") between [Yan'an Elevated Road](/wiki/Yan%27an_Elevated_Road "Yan'an Elevated Road") and [North–South Elevated Road](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Elevated_Road_(Shanghai) "North–South Elevated Road (Shanghai)")

Shanghai is a major hub of [China's expressway network](/wiki/Expressways_of_China "Expressways of China"). Many national expressways pass through or end in Shanghai, including [Jinghu Expressway](/wiki/G2_Beijing%E2%80%93Shanghai_Expressway "G2 Beijing–Shanghai Expressway"), [Hurong Expressway](/wiki/G42_Shanghai%E2%80%93Chengdu_Expressway "G42 Shanghai–Chengdu Expressway"), [Shenhai Expressway](/wiki/G15_Shenyang%E2%80%93Haikou_Expressway "G15 Shenyang–Haikou Expressway"), [Hushaan Expressway](/wiki/G40_Shanghai%E2%80%93Xi%27an_Expressway "G40 Shanghai–Xi'an Expressway"), [Huyu Expressway](/wiki/G50_Shanghai%E2%80%93Chongqing_Expressway "G50 Shanghai–Chongqing Expressway"), [Hukun Expressway](/wiki/G60_Shanghai%E2%80%93Kunming_Expressway "G60 Shanghai–Kunming Expressway"), and [Shanghai Ring Expressway](/wiki/G1503_Shanghai_Ring_Expressway "G1503 Shanghai Ring Expressway").[[217]](#cite_note-expresswayshanghai-226) There are also numerous municipal expressways prefixed with the letter S.[[217]](#cite_note-expresswayshanghai-226) As of 2019, Shanghai has 12 bridges and 14 tunnels crossing the Huangpu River.[[218]](#cite_note-227)[[219]](#cite_note-228)

Bicycle lanes are common in Shanghai, separating non-motorized traffic from car traffic on most surface streets. However, bicycles and motorcycles are banned on expressways and some main roads. Cycling has increased in popularity due to the emergence of dockless, app-based [bicycle-sharing systems](/wiki/Bicycle-sharing_system "Bicycle-sharing system"), such as [Mobike](/wiki/Mobike "Mobike"), [Hello](/wiki/Hello_(Chinese_company) "Hello (Chinese company)"), and [DiDi Bike](/w/index.php?title=DiDi_Bike&action=edit&redlink=1 "DiDi Bike (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%BB%B4%E6%BB%B4%E9%9D%92%E6%A1%94 "zh:滴滴青桔")].[[220]](#cite_note-229)[[221]](#cite_note-230) As of December 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), bicycle-sharing systems had an average of 1.15 million daily riders within the city.[[222]](#cite_note-231)

Private car ownership in Shanghai is rapidly increasing: in 2019, there were 3.40 million private cars in the city, a 12.5% increase from 2018.[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141) New private cars cannot be driven without a [license plate](/wiki/License_plate "License plate"), which are sold in monthly license plate auctions. Around 9,500 license plates are auctioned each month, and the average price was about CN¥89,600 (US$12,739) in 2019.[[223]](#cite_note-232) This policy was introduced to limit the growth of automobile traffic and alleviate congestion.[[224]](#cite_note-233)

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Railways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Shanghai_Railway_Station_4.jpg/250px-Shanghai_Railway_Station_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_Railway_Station_4.jpg)

[Shanghai railway station](/wiki/Shanghai_railway_station "Shanghai railway station")

Shanghai has four major railway stations: [Shanghai railway station](/wiki/Shanghai_railway_station "Shanghai railway station"), [Shanghai South railway station](/wiki/Shanghai_South_railway_station "Shanghai South railway station"), [Shanghai West railway station](/wiki/Shanghai_West_railway_station "Shanghai West railway station"), and [Shanghai Hongqiao railway station](/wiki/Shanghai_Hongqiao_railway_station "Shanghai Hongqiao railway station").[[225]](#cite_note-234)

Built in 1876, the [Woosung railway](/wiki/Woosung_Road "Woosung Road") was the first railway in Shanghai and the first railway in operation in China[[226]](#cite_note-235) By 1909, [Shanghai–Nanjing railway](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Nanjing_railway "Shanghai–Nanjing railway") and [Shanghai–Hangzhou railway](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Hangzhou_railway "Shanghai–Hangzhou railway") were in service.[[227]](#cite_note-236)[[228]](#cite_note-237) As of October 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), the two railways have been integrated into two main railways in China: [Beijing–Shanghai railway](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Shanghai_railway "Beijing–Shanghai railway") and [Shanghai–Kunming railway](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Kunming_railway "Shanghai–Kunming railway"), respectively.[[229]](#cite_note-238)

Shanghai is a terminus for five high-speed railways (HSRs): [Beijing–Shanghai HSR](/wiki/Beijing%E2%80%93Shanghai_high-speed_railway "Beijing–Shanghai high-speed railway") (overlaps with [Shanghai–Wuhan–Chengdu passenger railway](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Wuhan%E2%80%93Chengdu_passenger_railway "Shanghai–Wuhan–Chengdu passenger railway")), [Shanghai–Nanjing intercity railway](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Nanjing_intercity_railway "Shanghai–Nanjing intercity railway"), [Shanghai–Kunming HSR](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Kunming_high-speed_railway "Shanghai–Kunming high-speed railway"), [Shanghai–Nantong railway](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Nantong_railway "Shanghai–Nantong railway"), and [Shanghai–Suzhou–Huzhou HSR](/wiki/Shanghai%E2%80%93Suzhou%E2%80%93Huzhou_high-speed_railway "Shanghai–Suzhou–Huzhou high-speed railway").

Shanghai also has four [commuter railways](/wiki/Shanghai_Metropolitan_Area_Intercity_Railway "Shanghai Metropolitan Area Intercity Railway"): [Pudong railway](/wiki/Pudong_railway "Pudong railway") (although passenger service was suspended in 2015) and [Jinshan railway](/wiki/Jinshan_railway "Jinshan railway") operated by [China Railway](/wiki/China_Railway "China Railway"), and [Line 16](/wiki/Line_16_(Shanghai_Metro) "Line 16 (Shanghai Metro)") and [Line 17](/wiki/Line_17_(Shanghai_Metro) "Line 17 (Shanghai Metro)") operated by Shanghai Metro.[[230]](#cite_note-envir20160218-239)[[231]](#cite_note-envir20160418-240) As of January 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), four additional lines—[Chongming line](/wiki/Chongming_line "Chongming line"), [Jiamin line](/wiki/Jiamin_line "Jiamin line"), [Airport link line](/wiki/Airport_Link_line_(Shanghai) "Airport Link line (Shanghai)") and [Lianggang Express line](/wiki/Lianggang_Express_line "Lianggang Express line")—are under construction.[[231]](#cite_note-envir20160418-240)[[232]](#cite_note-241)

### Air and sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Air and sea")]

See also: [Port of Shanghai](/wiki/Port_of_Shanghai "Port of Shanghai")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Shanghai_Pudong_International_Airport_Interior.jpg/250px-Shanghai_Pudong_International_Airport_Interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_Pudong_International_Airport_Interior.jpg)

Inside Shanghai Pudong International Airport Terminal 1

Shanghai is one of the largest air transportation hubs in Asia.[[233]](#cite_note-242) The city has two commercial airports: [Shanghai Pudong International Airport](/wiki/Shanghai_Pudong_International_Airport "Shanghai Pudong International Airport") and [Shanghai Hongqiao International Airport](/wiki/Shanghai_Hongqiao_International_Airport "Shanghai Hongqiao International Airport").[[234]](#cite_note-243) Pudong is the primary international airport, while Hongqiao mainly operates domestic flights with limited short-haul international flights. In 2018, Pudong International Airport served 74.0 million passengers and handled 3.8 million tons of cargo, making it the ninth-busiest airport by passenger volume and third-busiest airport by cargo volume.[[235]](#cite_note-aerodata-244)[[236]](#cite_note-245) The same year, Hongqiao International Airport served 43.6 million passengers, making it the 19th-busiest airport by passenger volume.[[235]](#cite_note-aerodata-244)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Yangshan-Port-Balanced.jpg/250px-Yangshan-Port-Balanced.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yangshan-Port-Balanced.jpg)

Due to [Yangshan Port](/wiki/Yangshan_Port "Yangshan Port"), Shanghai has become the world's busiest container port.

Since its opening, the [Port of Shanghai](/wiki/Port_of_Shanghai "Port of Shanghai") has become the largest port in China.[[237]](#cite_note-246) Yangshan Port was built in 2005 because the river was unsuitable for docking large [container ships](/wiki/Container_ships "Container ships"). The port is connected with the mainland through the 32-kilometer (20 mi) long [Donghai Bridge](/wiki/Donghai_Bridge "Donghai Bridge"). In 2010, it became [world's busiest container port](/wiki/List_of_world%27s_busiest_container_ports "List of world's busiest container ports") with an annual [TEU](/wiki/Twenty-foot_equivalent_unit "Twenty-foot equivalent unit") transportation of 42 million in 2018.[[238]](#cite_note-247)[[239]](#cite_note-Lloyd's2019-248) The Port of Shanghai also handled 259 cruises and 1.89 million passengers in 2019.[[132]](#cite_note-SHECO2019-141) Although the port is run by the [Shanghai International Port Group](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Port_Group "Shanghai International Port Group") under the government of Shanghai, it administratively belongs to [Shengsi County](/wiki/Shengsi_County "Shengsi County"), Zhejiang.[[240]](#cite_note-249) Shanghai is part of the [21st Century Maritime Silk Road](/wiki/21st_Century_Maritime_Silk_Road "21st Century Maritime Silk Road") that runs from the Chinese coast to the northern Italian hub of [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste").[[241]](#cite_note-250)[[242]](#cite_note-251)[[243]](#cite_note-252)[[244]](#cite_note-253)[[245]](#cite_note-254)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Shanghai](/wiki/Culture_of_Shanghai "Culture of Shanghai")

The culture of Shanghai was formed by a combination of the [Wuyue culture](/wiki/Wuyue_culture "Wuyue culture") and the "East Meets West" [Haipai](/wiki/Haipai "Haipai") culture. Wuyue culture's influence is manifested in Shanghainese language—which comprises dialectal elements from Jiaxing, Suzhou, and Ningbo—and [Shanghai cuisine](/wiki/Shanghai_cuisine "Shanghai cuisine"), which was influenced by those of [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu_cuisine "Jiangsu cuisine") and [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang_cuisine "Zhejiang cuisine").[[246]](#cite_note-255) Haipai culture emerged after Shanghai became a prosperous port in the early 20th century, with foreigners from Europe, America, Japan, and India moving into the city.[[247]](#cite_note-256) The culture fuses elements of [Western cultures](/wiki/Western_cultures "Western cultures") with the local Wuyue culture, and its influence extends to the city's literature, fashion, architecture, music, and cuisine.[[248]](#cite_note-The_Culture_of_Shanghai._Beijing-257) The term Haipai was coined by Beijing writers in 1920 to criticize Shanghai scholars for admiring [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") and Western culture.[[248]](#cite_note-The_Culture_of_Shanghai._Beijing-257)[[249]](#cite_note-258) In the early 21st century, Shanghai has been recognized as a new influence and inspiration for [cyberpunk](/wiki/Cyberpunk "Cyberpunk") culture.[[250]](#cite_note-259) The city is recognized by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as a "[City of Design](/wiki/Design_Cities_(UNESCO) "Design Cities (UNESCO)")" since February 2010.[[251]](#cite_note-260)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Museums")]

See also: [List of museums in China § Shanghai](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_China#Shanghai "List of museums in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/China_Art_Museum_1.jpg/250px-China_Art_Museum_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_Art_Museum_1.jpg)

The China Art Museum, located in [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong "Pudong")

Cultural curation in Shanghai has grown since 2013, with several new museums having been opened in the city.[[252]](#cite_note-261) This is in part due to the city's 2018 development plans, which aim to make Shanghai "an excellent global city".[[253]](#cite_note-262) The Shanghai Museum has one of the largest collections of Chinese artifacts in the world, including a large collection of [ancient Chinese bronzes](/wiki/Ancient_Chinese_bronze "Ancient Chinese bronze") and [ceramics](/wiki/Chinese_ceramics "Chinese ceramics").[[254]](#cite_note-263) The [China Art Museum](/wiki/China_Art_Museum,_Shanghai "China Art Museum, Shanghai") is one of the largest museums in Asia and displays an animated replica of the 12th century painting [Along the River During the Qingming Festival](/wiki/Along_the_River_During_the_Qingming_Festival "Along the River During the Qingming Festival").[[255]](#cite_note-264) The [Shanghai Natural History Museum](/wiki/Shanghai_Natural_History_Museum "Shanghai Natural History Museum") and the [Shanghai Science and Technology Museum](/wiki/Shanghai_Science_and_Technology_Museum "Shanghai Science and Technology Museum") are natural history and science museums. There are numerous smaller, specialist museums housed in archeological and historical sites, such as the [Songze](/wiki/Songze_culture "Songze culture") Museum,[[256]](#cite_note-265) the [Site of the First National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Site_of_the_First_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Site of the First National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party"), the site of the former [Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_the_Republic_of_Korea "Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea"),[[257]](#cite_note-266) the Shanghai Jewish Refugees Museum, and the Shanghai Post Office Museum (located in the [General Post Office Building](/wiki/General_Post_Office_Building,_Shanghai "General Post Office Building, Shanghai")).[[258]](#cite_note-267)

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Shanghainese cuisine](/wiki/Shanghainese_cuisine "Shanghainese cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Xiao_Long_Bao_at_Nanxiang_Mantou_Dian_1.jpg/250px-Xiao_Long_Bao_at_Nanxiang_Mantou_Dian_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xiao_Long_Bao_at_Nanxiang_Mantou_Dian_1.jpg)

[Xiaolongbao](/wiki/Xiaolongbao "Xiaolongbao") in Shanghai

Benbang cuisine (本帮菜)[[259]](#cite_note-kankanews-268) is cooking style that originated in the 1600s, with influences from surrounding provinces. It emphasizes the use of condiments while retaining the original flavors of the raw ingredients. Sugar is an important ingredient in Benbang cuisine, especially in combination with soy sauce. Signature dishes of Benbang cuisine include [Xiaolongbao](/wiki/Xiaolongbao "Xiaolongbao"), [Red braised pork belly](/wiki/Red_braised_pork_belly "Red braised pork belly"), and [Shanghai hairy crab](/wiki/Chinese_mitten_crab "Chinese mitten crab").[[260]](#cite_note-269)

[Haipai cuisine](/wiki/Haipai_cuisine "Haipai cuisine") is a Western-influenced cooking style that originated in Shanghai. It uses elements from French, British, Russian, German, and Italian cuisines and adapted them for local taste preferences and to incorporate local ingredients.[[261]](#cite_note-270) Haipai cuisine dishes include Shanghai-style [borscht](/wiki/Borscht "Borscht") (罗宋汤, "Russian soup"), crispy pork cutlets, and [Shanghai salad](/wiki/Shanghai_salad "Shanghai salad"), derived from [Olivier salad](/wiki/Olivier_salad "Olivier salad").[[262]](#cite_note-271) Both Benbang and Haipai cuisine use various seafoods including [freshwater fish](/wiki/Freshwater_fish "Freshwater fish"), [shrimp](/wiki/Shrimp "Shrimp"), and crab.[[263]](#cite_note-272)

### Visual arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Visual arts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Renxiong_wan04s.jpg/250px-Renxiong_wan04s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Renxiong_wan04s.jpg)

十万图之四 (*No. 4 of a Hundred Thousand Scenes*) by [Ren Xiong](/wiki/Ren_Xiong "Ren Xiong"), a pioneer of the Shanghai School of Chinese art, c. 1850

The Songjiang School (淞江派), containing the Huating School (华亭派) founded by [Gu Zhengyi](/wiki/Gu_Zhengyi "Gu Zhengyi"),[[264]](#cite_note-273) was a small painting school in Shanghai during the Ming and Qing Dynasties.[[265]](#cite_note-274) It was represented by [Dong Qichang](/wiki/Dong_Qichang "Dong Qichang").[[266]](#cite_note-275) The school was considered an expansion of the [Wu School](/wiki/Wu_School "Wu School") in Suzhou, the cultural center of the Jiangnan region at the time.[[267]](#cite_note-276) The [Shanghai School](/wiki/Haipai "Haipai") commenced in the 19th century, focusing on the visual content of painting through the use of bright colors, using secular objects like flowers and birds as themes.[[268]](#cite_note-277) [Western art](/wiki/Western_art "Western art") was introduced to Shanghai in 1847 by Spanish missionary Joannes Ferrer (范廷佐), and the city's first Western atelier was established in 1864 inside the [Tushanwan orphanage](/wiki/Tushanwan "Tushanwan") (土山湾孤儿院).[[269]](#cite_note-278) During the Republic of China, artists including [Zhang Daqian](/wiki/Zhang_Daqian "Zhang Daqian"), [Liu Haisu](/wiki/Liu_Haisu "Liu Haisu"), [Xu Beihong](/wiki/Xu_Beihong "Xu Beihong"), [Feng Zikai](/wiki/Feng_Zikai "Feng Zikai"), and [Yan Wenliang](/wiki/Yan_Wenliang "Yan Wenliang") settled in Shanghai, allowing it to become the art center of China. Art forms such as [photography](/wiki/Photography "Photography"), [wood carving](/wiki/Wood_carving "Wood carving"), [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture"), [comics](/wiki/Comics "Comics") ([Manhua](/wiki/Manhua "Manhua")), and [Lianhuanhua](/wiki/Lianhuanhua "Lianhuanhua")—thrived. [Sanmao](/wiki/Sanmao_(comics) "Sanmao (comics)") was created to dramatize the chaos created by the Second Sino-Japanese War.[[270]](#cite_note-279) The most comprehensive art and cultural facility in Shanghai is the China Art Museum, with 64,000 square metres (690,000 sq ft) of exhibition space.[[271]](#cite_note-280)[[272]](#cite_note-281)

Since 2001, Shanghai has held [Shanghai Fashion Week](/wiki/Shanghai_Fashion_Week "Shanghai Fashion Week") each April and October. The main venue is in [Fuxing Park](/wiki/Fuxing_Park "Fuxing Park"), and the opening and closing ceremonies are held in the Shanghai Fashion Center. The April session is also part of the one-month [Shanghai International Fashion Culture Festival](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_International_Fashion_Culture_Festival&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shanghai International Fashion Culture Festival (page does not exist)").[[273]](#cite_note-282)

### Performance arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Performance arts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Mei_Lanfang_performing_at_Tianchan_Theatre.jpg/250px-Mei_Lanfang_performing_at_Tianchan_Theatre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mei_Lanfang_performing_at_Tianchan_Theatre.jpg)

[Mei Lanfang](/wiki/Mei_Lanfang "Mei Lanfang") performing the [Peking opera](/wiki/Peking_opera "Peking opera") "Resisting the Jin Army" at [Tianchan Theatre](/wiki/Tianchan_Theatre "Tianchan Theatre")

Traditional [Chinese opera](/wiki/Chinese_opera "Chinese opera") became a popular source of public entertainment in the late 19th century. In the early 20th century, [monologue](/wiki/Monologue "Monologue") and [burlesque](/wiki/Burlesque "Burlesque") in Shanghainese appeared, absorbing elements from traditional dramas. In the 1920s, [Pingtan](/wiki/Suzhou_Pingtan "Suzhou Pingtan") performance art expanded from Suzhou to Shanghai;[[274]](#cite_note-283) commercial radio stations expanded its popularity in the 1930s, with 103 programs every day. A Shanghai-style Beijing Opera was formed in the 1930s, led by [Zhou Xinfang](/wiki/Zhou_Xinfang "Zhou Xinfang") and [Gai Jiaotian](/w/index.php?title=Gai_Jiaotian&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gai Jiaotian (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%9B%96%E5%8F%AB%E5%A4%A9 "zh:盖叫天")].[[275]](#cite_note-284) A small troupe from Shengxian (now [Shengzhou](/wiki/Shengzhou "Shengzhou")) promoted [Yue opera](/wiki/Yue_opera "Yue opera") on the Shanghainese stage.[[276]](#cite_note-285) [Shanghai opera](/wiki/Shanghai_opera "Shanghai opera") was formed when local folksongs were fused with modern operas.[[277]](#cite_note-286)

[Drama](/wiki/Drama "Drama") appeared in [missionary schools](/wiki/Mission_school "Mission school") in Shanghai in the late 19th century, mainly performed in English. *Scandals in Officialdom* (官场丑史), staged in 1899, was one of the earliest-recorded plays.[[278]](#cite_note-287) In 1907, *[Uncle Tom's Cabin; or, Life Among the Lowly](/wiki/Uncle_Tom%27s_Cabin;_or,_Life_Among_the_Lowly "Uncle Tom's Cabin; or, Life Among the Lowly")* (黑奴吁天录) was performed at the [Lyceum Theatre](/w/index.php?title=Lyceum_Theatre,_Shanghai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lyceum Theatre, Shanghai (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%85%B0%E5%BF%83%E5%A4%A7%E6%88%8F%E9%99%A2 "zh:兰心大戏院")].[[279]](#cite_note-288)

Shanghai is the birthplace of [Chinese cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_China "Cinema of China");[[280]](#cite_note-289) China's first short film, *[The Difficult Couple](/wiki/The_Difficult_Couple "The Difficult Couple")* (1913), and the country's first fictional feature film, *[An Orphan Rescues His Grandfather](/wiki/An_Orphan_Rescues_His_Grandfather "An Orphan Rescues His Grandfather")* (孤儿救祖记, 1923)[[281]](#cite_note-290) were both produced in the city. Shanghai's film industry grew during the early 1930s, generating stars such as [Hu Die](/wiki/Hu_Die "Hu Die"), [Ruan Lingyu](/wiki/Ruan_Lingyu "Ruan Lingyu"), [Zhou Xuan](/wiki/Zhou_Xuan "Zhou Xuan"), [Jin Yan](/wiki/Jin_Yan "Jin Yan"), and [Zhao Dan](/wiki/Zhao_Dan "Zhao Dan"). The exile of Shanghainese filmmakers and actors during the Second Sino-Japanese War and the [Communist revolution](/wiki/Communist_revolution "Communist revolution") contributed to the development of the [Hong Kong film industry](/wiki/Hong_Kong_film_industry "Hong Kong film industry").[[282]](#cite_note-SHHKFilm-291) [Shanghai Television Festival](/wiki/Shanghai_Television_Festival "Shanghai Television Festival"), founded in 1986, is the earliest international TV festival founded in China. The [Shanghai International Film Festival](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Film_Festival "Shanghai International Film Festival") was founded in 1993 and is one of the nine major international film festivals in the A category.[[283]](#cite_note-292)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Shanghai_F1_Circui_01.jpg/250px-Shanghai_F1_Circui_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_F1_Circui_01.jpg)

F1 Chinese Grand Prix in Shanghai

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Tsonga_Potro_2008_Tennis_Masters.jpg/250px-Tsonga_Potro_2008_Tennis_Masters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tsonga_Potro_2008_Tennis_Masters.jpg)

Shanghai Masters in [Qizhong Forest Sports City Arena](/wiki/Qizhong_Forest_Sports_City_Arena "Qizhong Forest Sports City Arena")

Shanghai has several [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") teams, including two in the [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League"): [Shanghai Shenhua](/wiki/Shanghai_Shenhua_F.C. "Shanghai Shenhua F.C.")[[284]](#cite_note-绿地宣布接手申花_朱骏时代宣告终结-293) and [Shanghai Port](/wiki/Shanghai_Port_F.C. "Shanghai Port F.C.").[[285]](#cite_note-294) Shanghai's top-tier [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") team, the [Shanghai Sharks](/wiki/Shanghai_Sharks "Shanghai Sharks") of the [Chinese Basketball Association](/wiki/Chinese_Basketball_Association "Chinese Basketball Association"), developed [Yao Ming](/wiki/Yao_Ming "Yao Ming") before he entered the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association").[[286]](#cite_note-295)[[287]](#cite_note-yao_ming-296) Shanghai's baseball team, the [Shanghai Golden Eagles](/wiki/Shanghai_Golden_Eagles "Shanghai Golden Eagles"), plays in the [China Baseball League](/wiki/China_Baseball_League "China Baseball League").[[288]](#cite_note-297) Professional athletes from Shanghai include [110 metres hurdles](/wiki/110_metres_hurdles "110 metres hurdles") runner [Liu Xiang](/wiki/Liu_Xiang_(hurdler) "Liu Xiang (hurdler)"),[[289]](#cite_note-298) [table tennis](/wiki/Table_tennis "Table tennis") player [Wang Liqin](/wiki/Wang_Liqin "Wang Liqin"),[[290]](#cite_note-299) and [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") player [Wang Yihan](/wiki/Wang_Yihan "Wang Yihan").[[291]](#cite_note-300)

The [Shanghai Cricket Club](/wiki/Shanghai_Cricket_Club "Shanghai Cricket Club") dates back to 1858, when the first recorded cricket match was played between a team of [British Naval](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") officers and a Shanghai 11. The [Shanghai cricket team](/wiki/Shanghai_cricket_team "Shanghai cricket team") played various international matches between 1866 and 1948 as China's de facto [China national cricket team](/wiki/China_national_cricket_team "China national cricket team"). After going dormant in 1949 after the founding of the PRC, the club was re-established in 1994 by expatriates living in the city and has since grown to over 300 members.[[292]](#cite_note-301)

Shanghai hosts several international sports events. Since 2004, it has hosted the [Chinese Grand Prix](/wiki/Chinese_Grand_Prix "Chinese Grand Prix"), a round of the [Formula One World Championship](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One"), at the [Shanghai International Circuit](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Circuit "Shanghai International Circuit").[[293]](#cite_note-302) The city also hosts the [Shanghai Masters](/wiki/Shanghai_Masters_(tennis) "Shanghai Masters (tennis)") tennis tournament, which is part of [ATP World Tour Masters 1000](/wiki/ATP_World_Tour_Masters_1000 "ATP World Tour Masters 1000"), as well as golf tournaments including the [BMW Masters](/wiki/BMW_Masters "BMW Masters") and [WGC-HSBC Champions](/wiki/WGC-HSBC_Champions "WGC-HSBC Champions").[[294]](#cite_note-303) In 2023, Shanghai hosted 118 sports events, with 190,000 participants and 1.29 million spectators, driving a consumption of CN¥3.713 billion (US$510.83 million).[[295]](#cite_note-304)

## Environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Environment")]

### Parks and resorts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Parks and resorts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Shanghai_disneyland_castle.jpg/250px-Shanghai_disneyland_castle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_disneyland_castle.jpg)

Enchanted Storybook Castle of [Shanghai Disneyland](/wiki/Shanghai_Disneyland "Shanghai Disneyland")

Shanghai has an extensive public park system; by 2022, the city had 670 parks, of which 281 had free admission, and the per capita park area was 9 m2 (97 sq ft).[[296]](#cite_note-Shanghai_Overview_2019-305) The largest park in Shanghai is [Century Park](/wiki/Century_Park_(Shanghai) "Century Park (Shanghai)") in Pudong.[[297]](#cite_note-306)

The [People's Square](/wiki/People%27s_Square_(Shanghai) "People's Square (Shanghai)") park, located in the heart of downtown Shanghai, is known for its proximity to other major landmarks in the city. [Fuxing Park](/wiki/Fuxing_Park "Fuxing Park"), located in the former French Concession, features formal French-style gardens and is surrounded by high-end bars and cafes.[[298]](#cite_note-307) [Lu Xun Park](/wiki/Lu_Xun_Park "Lu Xun Park") in Hongkou is named after writer [Lu Xun](/wiki/Lu_Xun "Lu Xun"), whose tomb is located within the park.[[299]](#cite_note-308) [Zhongshan Park](/wiki/Zhongshan_Park_(Shanghai) "Zhongshan Park (Shanghai)"), in western central Shanghai, contains a monument of [Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin"), the tallest statue dedicated to the composer in the world.[[300]](#cite_note-309) The park features [sakura](/wiki/Cherry_blossom "Cherry blossom") and [peony](/wiki/Peony "Peony") gardens and a 150-year-old [platanus](/wiki/Platanus "Platanus").[[301]](#cite_note-310)

[Shanghai Botanical Garden](/wiki/Shanghai_Botanical_Garden "Shanghai Botanical Garden") is located 12 km (7 mi) southwest of the city center and established in 1978. In 2011, the largest botanical garden in Shanghai—[Shanghai Chen Shan Botanical Garden](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Chen_Shan_Botanical_Garden&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shanghai Chen Shan Botanical Garden (page does not exist)")—opened in Songjiang District.[[302]](#cite_note-311) The [Shanghai Disney Resort](/wiki/Shanghai_Disney_Resort "Shanghai Disney Resort") opened in 2016,[[303]](#cite_note-312) featuring a castle that is the biggest among Disney's resorts.[[304]](#cite_note-313)

### Air pollution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Air pollution")]

See also: [Pollution in China § Air pollution](/wiki/Pollution_in_China#Air_pollution "Pollution in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Shanghai_haze_in_Huangpu_Distract_20131206.jpg/250px-Shanghai_haze_in_Huangpu_Distract_20131206.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shanghai_haze_in_Huangpu_Distract_20131206.jpg)

[Huangpu District](/wiki/Huangpu_District,_Shanghai "Huangpu District, Shanghai") during the 2013 Eastern China smog

[Air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") in Shanghai is not as severe as in many other Chinese cities, but is still considered substantial by world standards.[[305]](#cite_note-314) During the [2013 Eastern China smog](/wiki/2013_Eastern_China_smog "2013 Eastern China smog"), air pollution rates reached between 23 and 31 times the international standard.[[306]](#cite_note-315)[[307]](#cite_note-Liu_Chen-yao-316) On 6 December 2013, levels of PM2.5 [particulate matter](/wiki/Particulates "Particulates") in Shanghai rose above 600 micrograms per cubic meter and in the surrounding area, above 700 micrograms per cubic meter.[[307]](#cite_note-Liu_Chen-yao-316) Levels of PM2.5 in Putuo District reached 726 micrograms per cubic meter.[[308]](#cite_note-317)[[309]](#cite_note-318) The following month, [Yang Xiong](/wiki/Yang_Xiong_(politician) "Yang Xiong (politician)"), the [mayor of Shanghai](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Shanghai "List of mayors of Shanghai"), announced three measures to manage the air pollution in Shanghai: implementing the 2013 air-cleaning program, establishing a linkage mechanism with the three surrounding provinces, and improving the city's early-warning systems.[[310]](#cite_note-yangxiongannouncement-319) That year, China's cabinet announced that a CN¥10 billion (US$1.7 billion) fund will be set up to help companies meet the new environmental standards.[[311]](#cite_note-320) From 2013 to 2018, more than 3,000 treatment facilities for industrial waste gases were installed, and the city's annual [smoke](/wiki/Smoke "Smoke"), [nitrogen oxide](/wiki/Nitrogen_oxide "Nitrogen oxide"), and [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide") emissions decreased by 65%, 54%, and 95%, respectively.[[312]](#cite_note-321)[[313]](#cite_note-322)

In 2023, the Air Quality Index (AQI) of Shanghai reached a rate of 87.7%, a 0.6% increase compared to the previous year. The annual average concentration of inhalable particulate matter (PM10) was 48 microgrammes per cubic meter, while the annual average concentration of fine particulate matter was 28 microgrammes per cubic meter.[[314]](#cite_note-323)

### Environmental protection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Environmental protection")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/A_residual_waste_truck_and_a_household_food_waste_truck_on_Zhonghua_Road%2C_Shanghai.jpg/250px-A_residual_waste_truck_and_a_household_food_waste_truck_on_Zhonghua_Road%2C_Shanghai.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_residual_waste_truck_and_a_household_food_waste_truck_on_Zhonghua_Road,_Shanghai.jpg)

A residual waste truck and a kitchen waste truck in [Huangpu](/wiki/Huangpu,_Shanghai "Huangpu, Shanghai")

A 16-year rehabilitation of Suzhou Creek, which runs through the city, was finished in 2012, clearing the creek of barges and factories and removing 1.3 million cubic meters of sludge.[[315]](#cite_note-324)[[316]](#cite_note-325) The government has moved almost all the factories within the city center to either the outskirts or other provinces.[[317]](#cite_note-airpollution-326) Shanghai once promoted the usage of [liquefied petroleum gas](/wiki/Liquefied_petroleum_gas "Liquefied petroleum gas") vehicles, such as scooters and taxis, in the early 2000s; due to safety risks and lack of refuelling stations, these vehicles met limited success in the city.[[318]](#cite_note-327)

On 1 July 2019, Shanghai adopted a new garbage-classification system that sorts waste into categories such as residual, kitchen, recyclable, and hazardous.[[319]](#cite_note-garbageclassification-328) The wastes are collected by separate vehicles and sent to [incineration](/wiki/Incineration "Incineration") plants, [landfills](/wiki/Landfill "Landfill"), [recycling](/wiki/Recycling "Recycling") centers, and hazardous-waste-disposal facilities, respectively.[[320]](#cite_note-329)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Media")]

[Media in Shanghai](/w/index.php?title=Media_in_Shanghai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Media in Shanghai (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E4%BC%A0%E5%AA%92%E4%B8%9A "zh:上海传媒业")] covers newspapers, publishers, broadcast, television, and the Internet, with some media having influence over the country. Concerning foreign publications in Shanghai, Hartmut Walravens of the [IFLA](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Library_Associations_and_Institutions "International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions") Newspapers Section said that when the Japanese controlled Shanghai in the 1940s "it was very difficult to publish good papers – one either had to concentrate on emigration problems, or cooperate like the *[Chronicle](/wiki/Shanghai_Jewish_Chronicle "Shanghai Jewish Chronicle")*."[[321]](#cite_note-330)

As of March 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit), newspapers publishing in Shanghai include:

* *[Jiefang Daily](/wiki/Jiefang_Daily "Jiefang Daily")*
* *[Oriental Sports Daily](/wiki/Oriental_Sports_Daily "Oriental Sports Daily")*
* *[Shanghai Review of Books](/wiki/Shanghai_Review_of_Books "Shanghai Review of Books")*
* *[Shanghai Daily](/wiki/Shanghai_Daily "Shanghai Daily")*
* *[Shanghai Star](/wiki/Shanghai_Star "Shanghai Star")*
* *[Xinmin Evening News](/wiki/Xinmin_Evening_News "Xinmin Evening News")*
* *[Wen Hui Bao](/wiki/Wen_Hui_Bao "Wen Hui Bao")*
* *[Wenhui Book Review](/wiki/Wenhui_Book_Review "Wenhui Book Review")*

Newspapers formerly published in Shanghai include:

* *[Der Ostasiatische Lloyd](/wiki/Der_Ostasiatische_Lloyd "Der Ostasiatische Lloyd")*
* *[Deutsche Shanghai Zeitung](/wiki/Deutsche_Shanghai_Zeitung "Deutsche Shanghai Zeitung")*
* *[Gelbe Post](/w/index.php?title=Gelbe_Post&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gelbe Post (page does not exist)")*[[322]](#cite_note-331)
* *[North China Daily News](/wiki/North_China_Daily_News "North China Daily News")*
* *[Shanghai Evening Post & Mercury](/wiki/Shanghai_Evening_Post_%26_Mercury "Shanghai Evening Post & Mercury")*
* *[The Shanghai Gazette](/w/index.php?title=The_Shanghai_Gazette&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Shanghai Gazette (page does not exist)")*[[323]](#cite_note-332)
* *[Shanghai Jewish Chronicle](/wiki/Shanghai_Jewish_Chronicle "Shanghai Jewish Chronicle")*
* *[Shanghai Herald](/wiki/Shanghai_Herald "Shanghai Herald")*
* *[The Shanghai Mercury](/w/index.php?title=The_Shanghai_Mercury&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Shanghai Mercury (page does not exist)")*[[324]](#cite_note-333)
* *[The Shanghai Post](/w/index.php?title=The_Shanghai_Post_(German-language_newspaper)&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Shanghai Post (German-language newspaper) (page does not exist)")*[[325]](#cite_note-334)
* *[Shanghai Times](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Times&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shanghai Times (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E6%B3%B0%E6%99%A4%E5%A3%AB%E6%8A%A5 "zh:上海泰晤士报")]*
* *[Shen Bao](/wiki/Shen_Bao "Shen Bao")*
* *[Israel's Messenger](/wiki/Israel%27s_Messenger "Israel's Messenger")*

The city's main broadcaster is [Shanghai Media Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Media_Group "Shanghai Media Group").

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: International relations")]

The city is the seat of the [New Development Bank](/wiki/New_Development_Bank "New Development Bank"), a [multilateral development bank](/wiki/Multilateral_development_bank "Multilateral development bank") established by the [BRICS](/wiki/BRICS "BRICS") [states](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state").
